segments.s310-g97: This phrase lists companions introduced by a prepositional/associative word (ابا), followed by coordinated proper names linked with و. In epic Persian an initial vowel may be added for meter; the conjunction و simply joins the names. طوس here is the hero Tous, not the city.
segments.s310-g98: The preposition ز + noun phrase پشت سپاه = 'from the rear (behind) of the army'; اندر آورد is a verb phrase meaning 'brought in / brought out' (آورد = brought) with اندر marking motion/placement into view; کوس is the direct object 'war-drum'. Word order is poetic (prepositional phrase before verb).
segments.s310-g99: دهاده is an emphatic/quantifying adjective meaning 'many / great (in number)' modifying the action expressed by برآمد 'arose/issued forth'. ز قلب سپاه is a source phrase 'from the heart/core of the army'. The verb برآمد is simple past/preterite 'rose/issued'.
segments.s310-g100: This is a symmetrical, elliptical construction: ز یک دست رستم ز یک دست شاه literally 'from one hand Rostam, from one hand the king' — ز = 'from', یک دست = 'one side/one hand' used idiomatically to mark opposing sides; word order omits a copula or verb for brevity, common in epic listing.
segments.s310-g101: شد here is the past copula/preterite 'became' or 'was made', followed by اندر هوا 'in the air' (location) and گرد بر سان میغ — گرد (here) conveys the idea of a circling/whirling mass or dust, بر سان = 'upon/like', میغ = 'blade'. The phrase paints a simile: the air turned into a whirling mass like a blade.
segments.s310-g102: چه میغی is an exclamatory noun phrase 'what a blade' (چه = exclamation); که introduces a relative/clausal remark 'whose/that', باران او = 'its shower/rain' (metaphorical), and تیر و تیغ = 'arrows and swords' as the things showered. The structure is poetic: exclamation + relative clause describing the metaphorical blade.
segments.s310-g103: تلی کشته = 'heaps of the slain' (تلی = a heap/pile, followed by the past-participle/adjunct کشته 'slain'); هر جای = 'every place, everywhere'; چون کوه کوه is a simile/repetition 'like mountain upon mountain' (چون = like, then the reduplication کوه کوه intensifies). Word order places the noun phrase before the simile for emphasis.
segments.s310-g104: زمین گشته combines زمین 'the earth' with گشته (perfect/agentless participle or intransitive 'has become / turned'), followed by از خون ایشان 'from their blood' indicating cause, and ستوه 'weary' as a predicative adjective. The sequence means 'the earth became weary from their blood' with گشته marking the change of state.
segments.s310-g129: می‌آورد is mi- + past stem (آورد) — in epic narrative it functions as a past/habitual action, here translated 'he brought'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. رامشگران is the plural object 'the comforters/entertainers' (object marked by را). را is the definite/object marker attached to the noun. بخواند is a 3rd-person past-form of خواندن with the older prefix b- (classical narrative past) meaning 'he called/summoned' here rather than 'read' or 'sing'. The whole clause is S (implicit subject) + compound predicates: 'he brought (them) and summoned the entertainers.'
segments.s310-g130: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here marking source/group). لشکر فراوان 'an abundant host/army' is a noun + adjective phrase functioning as the source phrase. سران را is the definite object 'the chiefs/leaders' with را marking them as direct object. بخواند is the same past narrative form 'he summoned/called.' Word order places the source phrase before the object and verb for emphasis: 'from the numerous host he called the chiefs.'
segments.s310-g131: شبی is a temporal adverb 'one night / at night' placed before the verb for temporal framing. کرد is the simple past 'he made/held.' جشنی is the direct object 'a celebration/feast.' که introduces a relative/clausal adjunct 'that...' and تا روز پاک is an adverbial phrase 'until the clear/pure day' (تا = 'until', روز = 'day', پاک = 'clear/pure'). Together: 'One night he held a celebration that lasted until the clear day.'
segments.s310-g132: همی is a classical emphatic/adverbial particle here meaning something like 'straightaway' or an emphatic marker for the ensuing action. مرده is the noun 'the dead / corpse(s)'. برخاست is the past tense 'rose up / arose.' از تیره خاک is a prepositional phrase 'from the dark earth/soil' (از = 'from', تیره = 'dark/bleak', خاک = 'earth/soil'). The clause describes the dead rising up from the earth immediately following the feast.
segments.s310-g133: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when.' خورشید is the subject 'the sun.' بر چرخ is a locative phrase 'upon the sky/wheel' (بر = 'on/upon', چرخ = 'sky/heaven' or cosmic 'wheel'). بنمود is the past form of 'to show/display' (ben- prefix + نمود) meaning 'showed' or 'turned/displayed.' پشت literally 'back' — here 'showed its back' (a poetic way to say the sun turned). The clause is a temporal clause: 'When the sun showed its back upon the sky...'
segments.s310-g134: شب تیره شد is 'the night became dark' (شب = night, تیره = dark, شد = became). از introduces the cause here ('because of/from') marking reason. نمودن here refers back to the 'appearance/showing' (the action of appearing/showing) and درشت is an adjective 'large/major' — together 'became dark because of that great appearing/manifestation.' Grammatically, this is a main clause reporting the effect (night darkened) and giving its cause with از + infinitival/masdar-like action.
segments.s310-g135: شهنشاه ایران is the subject 'the king/emperor of Iran.' سر و تن 'head and body' are the direct objects placed before the verb (a common classical word order for emphasis). بشست is the simple past 'he washed' (third person). The clause reads 'The emperor of Iran washed head and body' with implicit reflexive sense ('he washed his head and body').
segments.s310-g136: یکی here means 'a certain / one' (indefinite) and functions to introduce the following object. جایگاه پرستش is a noun phrase 'a place for worship' (جایگاه = place, پرستش = worship). بجست is the past tense 'he sought' (classical past form). Together: 'He sought a place of worship.' Note بجست can also mean 'leapt' in other contexts, but here the sense is 'sought.'
segments.s310-g161: وز آن یاوریها پشیمان شدند — The phrase begins with a prepositional/causal marker (وز, an older/poetic variant of از) followed by the demonstrative آن and the plural noun یاوریها, then the adjective پشیمان and the past-tense verb شدند. The verb شدند is the past/pluperfect-type copula meaning “became/turned (to be)”; word order is topic (circumstance) → subject → predicative adjective → finite verb, common in Persian poetry.
segments.s310-g162: پراندیشه دل سوی درمان شدند — پراندیشه is a compound adjective describing دل (lit. 'full of thought/care'); دل is the grammatical subject, سوی + درمان is a prepositional phrase 'toward (a) cure', and شدند again is the past copula 'became/turned'. The construction expresses a change of disposition: 'their hearts, made anxious/thoughtful, turned toward remedy.'
segments.s310-g163: همی گفت فغفور کافراسیاب — همی is a classical/periphrastic particle indicating continuous or habitual action in past narration ('kept/was saying'); گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided dictionary senses list omitted the verb sense). The title/name فغفور and epithet کافراسیاب follow as the speaker. Word order is particle + verb + speaker.
segments.s310-g164: از این پس نبیند بزرگی به خواب — از این پس is a temporal phrase 'from this time on / henceforth'; نبیند is a subjunctive/negative-future form 'will not see' (in narrative style); بزرگی is the object 'greatness'; به خواب is an adverbial phrase 'in a dream' (به + خواب). The clause expresses a future/habitual negation: 'he will not even in sleep see greatness.'
segments.s310-g165: ز لشکر فرستادن و خواسته — ز (poetic/short form of از) introduces the means/condition 'by/from'; لشکر فرستادن is a verbal noun phrase 'sending an army'; و links the next noun خواسته, here a noun meaning 'a demand/request'. The sequence lists the actions/conditions considered as causes.
segments.s310-g166: شود کار ما بی‌گمان کاسته — شود is a present/subjunctive auxiliary used here to form a future/passive sense 'will be' or 'will become'; کار ما is the patient/theme 'our affair/enterprise'; بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'; کاسته is the past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'diminished' and completes the predicate. Overall: 'our affair will undoubtedly be diminished.'
segments.s310-g167: پشیمانی آمد همه بهر ما — پشیمانی (regret) is the subject/noun; آمد is the past verb 'came' (used impersonally here: 'regret came'); همه 'all/everyone' modifies scope; بهر ما is a prepositional phrase 'for us/for our sake'. The clause means 'regret came to all for our (sake)' i.e. 'we all felt regret.'
segments.s310-g168: کز این کار ویران شود شهر ما — کز (classical contraction of که از/که ز) introduces a clause of consequence 'that/from which'; این کار 'this action/affair' is the cause; ویران is adjectival/predicative 'ruined'; شود is a subjunctive/auxiliary indicating result 'will be(come)'; شهر ما 'our city' is the patient. Together: 'that by this deed our city will be ruined.'
segments.s310-g17: بر این برنهادند و برخاستند is a two‑verb sequence in the simple past (3rd pl.). برنهادند (an older poetic verb) + بر means ‘set about / took up’ and here governs the following و + برخاستند ‘and they rose/rose up’. The prepositional phrase بر این (literally “upon/with this”) refers back to the foregoing matter and is placed before the verb, a common poetic word order.
segments.s310-g18: ز بهر ... بیاراستند expresses purpose: ز بهر = ‘for/for the sake of’ and introduces the goal of their action. بیاراستند is past 3rd‑pl. (literally “they adorned/arrayed”) used idiomatically here as ‘they made ready/prepared’ for شبیخون (‘a night‑raid’). The word order places the purpose phrase after the main clause.
segments.s310-g19: ز لشکر گزین کرد is an instance of classical Persian verb placement where the verbal complex گزین کرد (‘to choose’ + auxiliary/lexical کرد) follows the prepositional phrase ز لشکر (‘from the army’). The numeral/quantity (پنجه هزار) follows the verb as its object. گزین + کرد together form the past action ‘he selected/chose.’
segments.s310-g20: جهاندیده مردان خنجرگزار is a stacked noun phrase: جهاندیده (an adjective/epithet ‘worldly‑wise/seasoned’) modifies مردان (the men/warriors), followed by a further epithet خنجرگزار (‘dagger‑bearers’) describing their role. In Persian epithets commonly precede (or follow) the noun in poetry; here the sequence lists attributes of the selected troops.
segments.s310-g21: برفتند کارآگهان places the verb برفتند (past 3rd‑pl., ‘they went / proceeded’) before the noun کارآگهان (here a substantive meaning ‘the informed/knowledgeable ones’). پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase ‘before/in the presence of the king’ indicating goal/location; word order (verb → subject → prepositional phrase) is typical of epic verse.
segments.s310-g22: جهاندیده مردان با فر و جاه is an appositive or descriptive continuation: جهاندیده مردان (‘worldly‑wise men’) followed by با + noun phrase (با فر و جاه) ‘with glory and rank’ — با is the comitative/instrumental preposition linking the men to their attributes. فر here has the special meaning of royal splendour/charisma.
segments.s310-g23: ز کارآگهان آن‌که ... is a relative construction: ز کارآگهان ‘from/among the informants’ followed by the relative pronoun آن‌که ‘the one who’, which introduces a clause describing that specific person. بد رهنمای is a nominal phrase meaning ‘bad guide / one bringing bad counsel’ and serves as the predicate/description attached to آن‌که.
segments.s310-g24: بیامد به نزدیک پرده سرای is a motion clause in the past: بیامد ‘he came’ + a locative phrase به نزدیک ‘to/near’ and پرده سرای ‘the curtained chamber/inner apartment’. The verb is simple past; به نزدیک indicates approach, and پرده (curtain) + سرای names the place. (Note: the lemma senses given for سرای do not match the place‑meaning, so the sense for سرای is unspecified below.)
segments.s310-g25: به جایی is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'in/to any place' (here 'anywhere') placed before the noun phrase for emphasis. غو پاسبانان is a genitive/noun‑noun phrase 'the uproar/noise of the guards' functioning as the direct object. ندید is the past tense negative verb 'he did not see' (3rd‑person), so the whole clause reads 'he saw no clamor of the sentinels anywhere.'
segments.s310-g26: تو گفتی is a reporting clause: تو (2nd‑person 'you') + گفتی (past 2nd‑person 'you said'). The following clause is direct content of what was said. جهان سر به سر is an idiomatic expression where the repetition of سر (literally 'head') forms the adverbial 'completely / all over'; آرمید is past 'rested/slept' describing the state. Word order is subject (you) + verb (said) followed by the reported clause.
segments.s310-g27: This is a list of absent things using نه after each noun to negate their existence: طلایه نه 'no vanguard', و آتش 'and no fire', و باد نه 'and no wind'. The particle نه placed after each noun is a poetic/archaic negation marking 'there was not'.
segments.s310-g28: ز توران is a prepositional phrase 'from Turan' (ز = 'from'). کسی را is the definite/object phrase with را marking the object; in the negative context کسی functions as 'no one' / 'nobody'. به دل یاد نه is a verbless poetic clause where یاد 'remembrance' with به دل 'in the heart' is negated by the final نه — literally 'no one from Turan is (to) remembrance in the heart' i.e. 'no one from Turan is remembered in the heart.'
segments.s310-g29: چو آن دید is a temporal subordinate clause 'when he saw that' (چو = 'when'). برگشت و آمد is a sequence of past verbs 'he returned and came', and دوان is an adverbial/participle 'running/rushing' modifying آمد. The clause shows a quick, successive action: upon seeing it he turned and hurried back.
segments.s310-g30: کز introduces a content/explanatory clause 'that/for' (here giving the reason). ایشان 'they' is the genitive/subject, کسی in the negative environment with نیست means 'no one' (i.e. there is no one among them), and روشن‌روان is a compound adjective 'bright‑souled/clear‑spirited'. نیست is the present/persistent negative 'is not / there is not', so the clause reads 'for among them there is no one bright‑souled.'
segments.s310-g31: همه is the universal quantifier 'all'. خفتگان (the plural passive/agent noun from 'to sleep') means 'the sleeping' or 'those asleep' and here functions as the subject. سر به سر is an intensive idiom 'one and all / entirely', and مرده‌اند is the perfect/past participle construction 'they are dead / have died' (3rd‑person plural). The whole clause: 'all the sleepers, every one of them, are dead.'
segments.s310-g32: وگر نه is a conditional/adversative phrase 'if not / otherwise'. This introduces the counterfactual: همه روز 'all day / every day' and می‌خورده‌اند is the past continuous/conditional sense 'they would have been eating' (here implying 'otherwise, they would be eating all day'). The verb form expresses the hypothetical alternative to them being dead.
segments.s310-g185: فرستاده را گفت is literally 'he said to the messenger' — را marks the messenger as the recipient (similar to English 'to'). The verb گفت is past tense 'said'. کو را بگوی is a direct reported-order: کو 'where/who' (interrogative/relativizer) plus را (object/contrast marker) and بگوی, an imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'say' or 'tell'. Word order places the indirect object before the quoted instruction.
segments.s310-g186: که introduces the content of the command ('that' / 'that he should...'). خیره is a nominal/adjectival participle 'staring' or 'a stare' and functions as the cause of the action. بر ما is the prepositional phrase 'upon us' (target of the stare). مبر is the negative imperative 'do not take' (classical Persian negative imperative). آبروی, here without an explicit possessive suffix, means 'reputation/honor' — object of مبر.
segments.s310-g187: نباید که is an impersonal modal construction meaning 'it must not be that / should not' and introduces a subordinate clause. نزد تو is a locative phrase 'in your presence / near you' (نزد used in the sense 'in the presence of'). افراسیاب is the person who is being discussed and comes after the locative phrase.
segments.s310-g188: بیاید is the present/subjunctive form 'come' used under the scope of نباید که ('should not come'). شب تیره هنگام خواب is a sequence of time modifiers: شب تیره 'the dark night' + هنگام 'at the time (of)' + خواب 'sleep/bedtime' — together 'at night in the dark, at the time of sleep' (i.e. while sleeping).
segments.s310-g189: فرستاده برگشت و آمد is two past verbs linked by و — 'the messenger returned and came'. This is simple past narrative sequence. چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = 'as/like', باد = 'wind') modifying the manner of his coming.
segments.s310-g190: به فغفور is the prepositional goal 'to the emperor' (به + title). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' placed before the object. پیامش is پیام + enclitic -ش 'his message'. بداد here functions as a past transitive verb 'gave/delivered' (an older/poetic past form). Note: the provided sense list for بداد did not include a 'give/deliver' sense, so that word is marked below as not matching the given senses.
segments.s310-g191: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard'. The subject فغفور follows the verb (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian): 'when the emperor heard'. هنگام خواب is an adverbial time phrase 'at the time of sleep / at bedtime' modifying the hearing.
segments.s310-g192: فرستاد is the past transitive verb 'he sent/dispatch(ed)'. کس is an indefinite 'someone' (object of sending). نزد افراسیاب is the prepositional phrase indicating destination 'to Afrasiab's presence' (نزد used as 'in the presence of / to'). The clause means 'he sent someone to Afrasiab.'
segments.s310-g145: وز here is a poetic contraction corresponding to و از (and from) linking this clause to what came before; آنجا is a locative adverb ‘there’; بیامد is simple past third-person singular ‘he came’; سوی is a preposition placed before its object meaning ‘toward’; تاج و تخت is a coordinated noun phrase ‘crown and throne’. Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase + verb.
segments.s310-g146: A string of predicate/adverbial adjectives describing the (omitted) subject ‘he’: خرامان is an adverbial adjective ‘with a proud/stately gait’; و links items; شادان modifies دل in the genitive-like compound شادانِ دل ‘joyful of heart’; نیکبخت is a postpositive adjective ‘fortunate’. These words function as manner/quality phrases rather than independent clauses.
segments.s310-g147: از ایرانیان provides the group ‘of the Iranians’; هرکه is the indefinite relative ‘whoever/whomever’; افگنده (a past participle/adjective) means ‘fallen/laid low’ or ‘smitten’; بود is the past-tense copula ‘was’. Together they mean ‘whoever of the Iranians had been laid low.’
segments.s310-g148: This clause gives two alternative states introduced by an archaic conditional/alternative pattern: اگر ... بودند is ‘if they had been (killed)’ and گر here functions as ‘or’; زنده بود is the simple past ‘was alive’. The whole phrase means ‘whether they had been killed or were alive.’
segments.s310-g149: از آن خاک is the source phrase ‘from that earth/soil’; آوند/آورد here is a past verb ‘brought’ and برداشتند is past plural ‘they removed/took up’. The sequence of two past verbs describes the action of retrieving (presumably bodies) from the ground.
segments.s310-g150: تن دشمنان is a noun–noun construction ‘the body(ies) of the enemies’; خوار is a postpositive adjective ‘despised/worthless’; بگذاشتند is past plural ‘they placed/laid (down)’ or ‘they left’. The phrase describes placing down the enemy bodies contemptuously.
segments.s310-g151: همه modifies the following noun phrase to mean ‘the whole / all of’; رزمگه is ‘battlefield’; دخمه‌ها is plural ‘sepulchres / tombs’; ساختند is past plural ‘they built’. The verb agrees plurally with the (implicit) builders and the noun is pluralized.
segments.s310-g152: از آن کشتگان is a source/genitive phrase ‘from those slain / of the slain’; چون here functions as a temporal/as connective ‘as/when / after’; بپرداختند is past plural of پرداختن meaning ‘they attended to / dealt with’ (here: disposed of or buried). The clause indicates that the tombs were made in relation to handling the slain.
segments.s310-g33: به جایی functions as an adverbial phrase meaning 'in/to any place' (here: 'anywhere'); طلایه is the noun 'vanguard' placed before the verbal predicate پدیدار نیست, where پدیدار (an adjectival/verb root 'to appear / be manifest') combines with نیست to form a negated existential/predicative clause 'does not appear'. Word order is locative/adverbial + noun + negated verb, a common poetic structure.
segments.s310-g34: کس ... نیست is an existential negative construction meaning 'there is no one'; آن خفتگان را is the definite object (آن = 'those/the') marked by the object marker را, and نگهدار is a noun 'guardian/keeper' used predicatively with نیست: 'there is no one who is guardian of those sleepers.' The order places the negated copular verb at the end, as typical in classical/epic Persian.
segments.s310-g35: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; the clause follows Subject (افراسیاب) + Object (این سخنها) + Verb (شنود). شنود is an archaic/epic 3rd-person singular form meaning 'he heard' (used where modern Persian would use past شنید), so the whole segment reads 'When Afrasiab heard these words.'
segments.s310-g36: به دلش اندرون is a nested locative: به دلش 'into his heart' with the possessive clitic ش = 'his', and اندرون 'inner part/within' specifying location. روشنایی is the noun 'brightness/light' serving as the subject of the verbal predicate فزود, an archaic past-form meaning 'increased/grew.' The phrase thus means 'brightness increased within his inner heart.'
segments.s310-g37: سپه را فرستاد is a transitive clause with سپه 'army' marked by the object marker را and the past verb فرستاد 'he sent.' The coordinating و links the next clause; خود برنشست is reflexive/emphatic 'he himself sat down / took his place' with برنشست as a past 3rd-person singular verb.
segments.s310-g38: میان یلی is a prepositional/locative phrase 'among the valiant (men)'; تاختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'the charging/charge' and is marked by را as a definite object; ببست is the past tense verb 'he closed / he stopped / he checked.' Grammatically this reads 'Among the warriors he stopped the charge,' with the verbal noun تاختن functioning as the object of ببست.
segments.s310-g39: برفتند گردان is a verb-first poetic order meaning 'the troop/column advanced' (برفتند = 'they went/advanced', گردان = 'troop, company'). چو دریای آب is a simile 'like a sea of water' modifying the way they advanced; چو here means 'as/like.'
segments.s310-g40: گرفتند is the past plural 'they seized / took'; گرفتند بر تاختن uses the preposition بر + verbal noun تاختن to mean 'they set upon / fell upon the charge' (i.e., began or seized the act of charging). The final بر شتاب is an adverbial phrase 'with haste / with speed' (بر used to mark the manner). Overall: 'they launched into the charge with speed.'
segments.s310-g73: گرفتند is a past-plural verb 'they took/ seized'. In this poetic order the verb appears before the objects; ژوپین و خنجر are the items taken. به کف is an adverbial phrase 'into the palm / in hand' (به here marks direction/placement into the hand and کف = palm/hand). The whole clause means 'they grasped gambesons and daggers in hand.'
segments.s310-g74: کشیدند is past-plural 'they drew / they drew up' used transitively here (to draw up a formation). لشکر is the object 'army/host'. سه فرسنگ صف is a quantified noun phrase 'a line (صف) three farsangs (فرسنگ) long' with numeral + measure before the head noun. Together: 'they drew up an army in a battle-line three farsangs long.'
segments.s310-g75: به کردار دریا is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner/behavior of the sea' (کردار here = manner/behavior). شد is past 'became'. آن رزمگاه is the subject ('that battlefield') placed after the verb for poetic word order. Literal: 'became, that battlefield, like the sea' → 'the battlefield became like the sea.'
segments.s310-g76: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. خورشید تابنده روشن strings noun + modifiers ('the shining sun [was] bright'); the copula/verb is omitted (understood from previous clause), so the phrase means 'neither the shining sun was bright nor the moon.'
segments.s310-g77: سپاه 'army' is the subject; اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'entered / came in' (اندر = in/into + آمد = came). همی is a poetic particle indicating continuous or iterative aspect ('kept coming' / 'indeed, continually'). فوج فوج repeats 'فوج' to mean 'troop after troop' (iterative distribution).
segments.s310-g78: بر آن سان که is a comparative phrase 'in such a manner that / as' (بر + آن سان + که). برخیزد is present/subjunctive 'that [it] may rise' or simply 'that rises' in the comparative clause. از باد موج is a prepositional phrase 'waves from the wind' (از = from, باد = wind, موج = wave). The clause compares the arriving troops to waves rising from the wind.
segments.s310-g79: در و دشت is an idiomatic pairing 'court/settlement and plain' meaning 'everywhere / in the towns and the fields'. گفتی here is parenthetical 'one might say' or 'so it was said' (a poetic discourse particle). همه خون شدست: همه 'all/everything', خون 'blood', شدست is an archaic/perfect-form 'has become' (third-person); together 'everything has become blood' (i.e. the ground is all blood).
segments.s310-g80: خور is 'the sun' (subject). از چرخ گردنده is a genitive/prepositional phrase 'from the revolving wheel' (چرخ here = heaven/sky, گردنده = rotating/revolving). بیرون شدست is a compound perfect-like form 'has gone out / gone away' (past/resultative). The clause says the sun has gone out from the turning heavens.
segments.s310-g289: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the noun phrase. گاوان گردون‌کشان is a noun plus a compound adjective: گردون ('the sky/heaven') + کشان ('‑pulling/‑dragging') forms an epithet 'heaven‑dragging' modifying گاوان (oxen/cattle). The numeral ده هزار follows the noun phrase as the quantity 'ten thousand'.
segments.s310-g290: ببردند is the past plural 'they carried/took away'. تا here is temporal 'until' introducing the subordinate clause. خود 'himself' is emphatic. کی in poetry means 'when'. آید is the present/optative stem 'come' (here used as future/expected arrival), به is the preposition 'to/into', and کار is 'the action/task' (here 'the fight' or 'the affair'); together تا ... آید به کار = 'until he himself comes into action.'
segments.s310-g291: This phrase places the source first: ز + گنج ('from the treasury'). هیونان (literally 'beasts/animals') here functions as the pack animals or the carriers. درم ده هزار is the quantified object 'ten thousand dirhams' with the numeral following the noun in this poetic word order.
segments.s310-g292: بسی is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'many/much' (used here to qualify بار). بار کردند is a light-verb construction 'they made/they loaded (loads)'; با + شهریار 'with the king' indicates accompaniment or that the loading was done in the king's service. The verb کردند is simple past plural.
segments.s310-g293: بفرمود is an honorific past-form verb 'he commanded/said'. زان پس is a temporal/sequence phrase 'after that'. به هنگام خواب is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time of sleep' (i.e., at night). This entire segment sets the time and the speech/commanding verb for the following subordinate clause.
segments.s310-g294: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/which'). پوشیده is an adjective 'covered'. پوشیده‌رویان (here written پوشیده رویان) is an epithet meaning 'those with covered faces' or 'having a covered face' placed before the proper name; it functions as a descriptive phrase attached to افراسیاب (Afrasiab). (The item رویان as listed in the provided senses does not match this usage exactly, so it is treated as the plural/nominal base 'faces' in the compound.)
segments.s310-g295: ز indicates 'from among'. خویشان و پیوند is a paired noun phrase 'kinsmen and kindred/ties' (پیوند here = family/kinship link). چندان که هست is a common formulaic clause 'as many as there are' or 'to the extent that they exist' — چندان functioning as an indefinitizing/quantifying adverb and هست the copula 'are/exist'.
segments.s310-g296: این جمله offers two alternatives introduced by اگر used in the sense 'whether/if'. دخترانند is a verbless predication 'they are daughters' (‑ند is the present plural copula). The second اگر modifies زیردست 'subordinates/dependents'; the construction means 'whether they be daughters or subordinates.'
segments.s310-g297: همه (subject) + در عماری (prepositional phrase) describes location: "all (of them) in/into a building/chamber." به راه آوردند is a verb phrase in simple past plural (آوردند = they brought/led) where به راه here means "into/along the way"; word order places the locative before the verb, typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s310-g298: ز ایوان (from the iwan/porch) is a poetic ablative with ز = "from." به میدان شاه is a directional phrase (به = to/toward + میدان شاه = the king's open space/courtyard). آوردند repeats the past plural verb "they brought/led (them)." The clause continues the same action with explicit source and destination.
segments.s310-g299: دو از نامداران گردنکشان is a noun phrase: دو (two) + از (of/from) selecting two individuals out of the group named by نامداران (renowned ones) modified by گردنکشان ("rebellious/proud ones"). The genitive-like use of از marks selection from a class.
segments.s310-g300: که بودند is a relative clause head (که = who/that) with the past verb بودند "they were." هر یک به مردی نشان functions as the predicate: هر یک = "each (one)," به مردی = "to/for a man," and نشان is a nominal predicate meaning "a token/trophy." The clause says each of the two served as a (personal) trophy for some man.
segments.s310-g301: چو functions as a comparative/simile particle meaning "like/as," repeated for two names: چو جهن و چو گرسیوز = "like Jahn and like Garsivaz." ارجمند is an adjective following the names meaning "esteemed/precious," qualitatively describing them. Word order places the simile before the following clause.
segments.s310-g302: به مهد اندرون is a locative phrase: به = to/toward, مهد اندرون = "the inner cradle/innermost chamber" (مهد here as a room/crib figuratively). پای کرده is a compound participial construction: پای (foot) + کرده (done, here "having set/placed the feet"); it indicates that their feet were put/placed. به بند (to bonds) indicates the result: their feet were put into fetters. The sequence describes being led inside and bound.
segments.s310-g303: همه خویش و پیوند افراسیاب lists affected relatives/groups: همه خویش = "all his own kin" (خوی + possessive ش = kin/his kin), و = and, پیوند افراسیاب = "the connections/relatives of Afrasiab." The phrase groups family/ties as the subject or topical phrase.
segments.s310-g304: ز تیمارشان is an ablative/causal phrase (ز = from/because of + تیمارشان = their care/solicitude). دیده کرده is a causative/perfect-like construction: دیده ("eye/eyes") + کرده ("made/done") = "had (his) eyes made (so)"; پر آب = "full of water." Together the clause means that because of their care his eyes were filled with water (i.e., he was moved to tears).
segments.s310-g137: کز ... تا / کز here is the old poetic preposition 'from/among'; the phrase 'کز ایرانیان' means 'from among the Iranians'. The core clause 'کس ... ندید' is negative: 'no one saw'. The enclitic 'مر' is an archaic dative 'to me' (i.e. 'to/for me' or 'to my knowledge') which indicates that nobody reported or came to tell the speaker that they had seen him; 'او را' is the definite object marker + pronoun 'him', and 'ندید' is the past form 'saw' (negated by the initial 'کس' here = 'no one').
segments.s310-g138: The second clause continues the negation with 'نه' (nor): 'نه ... شنید' = 'nor did (he/they) hear'. 'دام و دد' is a poetic pairing meaning 'traps and foes/hostile beasts'; 'آوای ایشان' = 'their sound/cry'; and 'شنید' is the past 'heard'. Together: 'nor did anyone hear the sound/cry of their traps and enemies.'
segments.s310-g139: ز ... تا introduces a range: 'from X to Y' — here 'from the شبگرد (night-watcher/night-watch) up to the ماه (moon)'. The phrase 'بر چرخ ساج' attaches to the moon and describes it 'on the wheel/skies like a saj' (a saj is a rounded metal/surface in poetry), so the whole prepositional cluster pictures the celestial setting.
segments.s310-g140: This is the main action: 'به سر بر نهاد' = 'he placed upon the head' (به سر = onto the head; بر نهاد = placed/set). 'آن دل‌افروز تاج' is the direct object placed on the head: 'that heart‑delighting crown' (آن = that; دل‌افروز = cheering/delightful; تاج = crown). The verb نهاد is past 'placed/put'.
segments.s310-g141: 'ستایش ... کرد' is the noun+verb construction meaning 'praised' (ستایش کردن). 'همی' is the archaic imperfective/continuative particle (kept/was), so 'ستایش همی کرد' = 'he kept praising' or 'he praised continually'. 'بر' here governs the beneficiary/target: 'بر کردگار' = 'to/unto the Creator' (i.e., praised God).
segments.s310-g142: This prepositional phrase gives the reason or occasion: 'از آن' = 'because of that' or 'on account of that'; 'شادمان' is an adjective 'joyful/rejoicing' modifying 'گردش روزگار' = 'the turn/cycle of fortune' (گردش = turning/cycle; روزگار = time/fate). So: 'because of that joyful turning of fate'.
segments.s310-g143: 'فراوان بمالید' = 'he rubbed plentifully/abundantly' (بمالید here = past of مالیدن 'to rub / to anoint / to smear'); the following prepositional phrase 'بر خاک روی' places the action on the object: 'his face upon the earth' ('بر خاک' = on the ground, 'روی' = face). The order is poetic but means 'he rubbed his face on the ground many times'.
segments.s310-g144: 'به رخ بر نهاد' = 'he placed upon the cheek' (به رخ = onto the cheek; بر نهاد = placed). The following noun phrase 'از دو دیده دو جوی' is literal and emphatic: 'two streams from two eyes' — a poetic way to mean tears flowing from both eyes. Word order places the locative phrase before the verb and the source/quantity after the verb.
segments.s310-g169: ز + Noun phrase marks origin/source here: 'ز چین و ختن' = 'from China and Khotan'. 'هدیه‌ها' is a plural direct object (no object marker needed) meaning 'gifts'. 'ساختند' is 3rd person plural past simple of ساختن — 'they made/fashioned'. Word order: prepositional source phrase, object, then verb (typical Persian clause order).
segments.s310-g170: 'بدان کار' = 'for that affair / for that purpose' (بدان is a fused deictic/prepositional unit pointing back to the mentioned action). 'گنجی' is an indef. treasure (object). 'بپرداختند' is 3rd person plural past of پرداختن with the prefix بـ- here meaning 'they paid/gave (money)'. The clause literally: 'for that purpose they paid a treasure.'
segments.s310-g171: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite/partitive noun phrase 'a certain envoy / an envoy' (the -ای ending signals 'one of' / 'a certain'). نیک‌دل is an adjectival epithet 'kind‑hearted' modifying the envoy. را is the definite/object marker signaling that the phrase is the object of the verb. بخواند here means 'he called/summoned' (from خواندن in the sense 'to call'), in 3rd person past/paraphrased narrative tense.
segments.s310-g172: سخنهای شایسته is 'appropriate words/speeches' (سخنهای = plural of 'speech/words', شایسته = 'appropriate, worthy'). چندی here means 'some/a few'. براند is the 3rd person present/past-form of راندن (here used idiomatically = 'puts forth / utters / drives forth'); in context it functions as 'he spoke/uttered' a few appropriate words. Word order: object phrase then verb as in Persian narrative.
segments.s310-g173: یکی مرد introduces an indefinite/particular 'a certain man'. The three following words are epithets: بد (an epithet whose nuance is ambiguous/archaic here), نیک‌دل 'kind‑hearted', نیک‌خواه 'well‑wisher'. In poetry multiple adjectives can stack without conjunctions; they jointly describe the man sent. The precise nuance of بد is context-dependent and can be idiomatic in classical Persian.
segments.s310-g174: فرستاد is 3rd person past singular/plural 'he/they sent'. فغفور is the addressee/title 'the Faghfur' (Byzantine emperor). نزدیک شاه is a locative/relational phrase 'near the king' or 'in the king's presence' qualifying the embassy or location. The clause reads: 'He sent [this man] to the Faghfur, near the king.'
segments.s310-g175: طرایف is a plural noun 'curiosities/oddities/wonders'. به چین اندرون 'in China, inside' places those wonders in the interior of China. آنچه بود is a relative clause 'that which was' / 'whatever there was'. Together: 'the curiosities inside China — whatever there was (of them)'. Note the implied subject/agent is narrative and can be supplied from context.
segments.s310-g176: ز دینار وز گوهر is a stock phrasing meaning 'of/with dinars and jewels'; here وز is the fused form (و + ز) meaning 'and from' / 'and of' (archaic/poetic). نابسود is used idiomatically to indicate they did not spare or were not stingy with these riches. The literal words: 'from dinars and from jewels — not spared.'
segments.s310-g201: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The prepositional phrases با درد و با رنج و غم ('with pain and with suffering and sorrow') qualify the situation of the following noun; they are placed before the verb to produce a poetic inversion. دید is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'he saw / perceived', and روز is the noun 'day' that is being perceived (word order here is somewhat inverted for meter: temporal/qualifying phrases + verb + noun).
segments.s310-g202: بیامد is past tense 'he came'. دمان here means the base/foothill (an older word for the skirt of a mountain). تا ... به marks extent: تا به کوه اسپروز = 'as far as (up to) Mount Asp-ruz'; تا به is idiomatic for 'to / as far as'.
segments.s310-g203: ز is the poetic/genitive/causal preposition 'from / because of'. بدخواه is 'ill-wisher' (someone hostile). روز و شب here functions adverbially ('day and night'), placed before the verb اندیشه کرد ('he thought / he pondered'), which is past tense ('کرد' is the light verb forming a compound with اندیشه).
segments.s310-g204: شب و روز را uses را as a topical/definite marker emphasizing 'the nights and days' (i.e., continually). دل ('heart') is the internal subject/experiencer and یکی پیشه کرد is a causative/result clause meaning 'he made (his) heart adopt one pursuit'—پیشه here means 'pursuit/practice/occupation' and یکی qualifies it as 'one, single'. The verb کرد is again the past light verb 'made/did'.
segments.s310-g205: بیامد is past tense 'he came'. ز چین is a prepositional phrase 'from China' (چین here is the place-name China). تا به introduces the endpoint 'as far as' and is followed by آب زره, literally 'the water (river) Zereh'—in classical Persian compound names the noun (آب) may precede a specific name or epithet (زره).
segments.s310-g206: میان begins an adverbial phrase 'amid / in the midst of'. سوده here is used as a past/participial/adjectival form (sense 'profited, availed' in the glosses) or more generally denotes a resulting state amid what follows; in context it links to the suffering that accompanies the journey. از introduces the cause/source 'from / by', and رنج و بند گره lists the accompanying miseries: رنج 'suffering', بند 'fetter/bond', گره 'knot'—together 'suffering and the knotted bonds'. The whole phrase describes his state amid hardships.
segments.s310-g207: چو is the temporal 'when'. نزدیک ... رسید is a verbal phrase 'came near / arrived near'; آن ژرف دریا is the noun phrase 'that deep sea' with the adjective ژرف ('deep') preceding the noun دریا (a common poetic order). رسید is the past-tense verb 'he reached/arrived'.
segments.s310-g208: این جمله uses a negative past verb ندید ('did not see'). آن را is the definite object with the object marker را ('that' as the thing referred to). میان و کرانه enumerate two parts of the sea ('middle and shore'), coordinated by و. مر is an older/pronunciation-variant pronoun form (used here in an emphatic/poetic role) and the clause as a whole means 'he did not see its middle nor its shore.'
segments.s310-g265: همی + past verb (گفت) is a literary continuous/habitual past: literally 'was saying' or emphatic 'said'. The verb precedes the subject (همی گفت رستم), a common classical Persian order. که here introduces the direct speech (equivalent to 'that' or the marker before quoting) and the following ای (written attached to که as که‌ای) is the vocative particle addressing the addressees: 'O...'. مهتران is the vocative plural 'chiefs/elders'.
segments.s310-g266: This is a nominal phrase with two attributive compounds (جهان‌دیده, رنجبرده) placed before the head noun سران; in poetic Persian such compound adjectives commonly precede or immediately attach to the noun they qualify. و simply links the two adjectives into a coordinated description of the leaders.
segments.s310-g267: نباید is an impersonal modal 'should/must not' that governs a subordinate clause introduced by که; the verb شود is in the 3rd‑person (subjunctive/jussive sense) 'become'. این رنج is the subject 'this suffering', and بی‌بر is a predicative adjective meaning 'without fruit/issue' (i.e. fruitless). The whole clause means 'it must not be that this suffering becomes fruitless.'
segments.s310-g268: به...اندر شود is a verbal phrase meaning 'to fall into / to become (subject to)'. Here به takes the sense 'into/towards' and is followed by two nouns linked by و (ناز و تن آسانی) 'pampering and bodily ease'; اندر شدَن ('enter into') expresses that the suffering be converted into indulgence. Word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb (typical in poetry).
segments.s310-g269: و دیگر functions as a connective 'and besides / moreover'. که introduces the content clause. این شاه is the noun phrase 'this king' and پیروزگر is an attributive adjective 'victorious / triumphant' modifying شاه; in Persian adjectives often follow the noun they modify in literary style.
segments.s310-g270: بیابد (third‑person jussive/subjunctive of یافتن) expresses a wish or hope here 'may he obtain/receive'; the enclitic همی adds durative/emphatic coloring ('always / ever'). ز + اختر نیک indicates source 'from an auspicious star', and the final بر is a small poetic particle giving a sense of 'over/above' (i.e. favour coming from the star above). Overall: 'may he ever obtain from a good/auspicious star.'
segments.s310-g271: از ایران برفتیم is a simple past narrative 'we departed from Iran' (verb برفتیم carries the subject 'we' in its ending). تا here means 'until' / 'as far as', and پیش marks the location 'before/in front of'. گنگ is used as a substantive (likely a name or epithet 'the mute one' or a place/person called Gank) and occupies the syntactic slot after پیش.
segments.s310-g272: ندیدیم is simple past negative 'we did not see'. جز introduces the exception 'except'. چنگ یازان is a compound nominal meaning 'the harp‑players' (literal components: چنگ 'harp' and یازان, a verbal/agentive form used here as 'those who play'), and به جنگ functions adverbially 'in/at the battle'. The phrase expresses 'we saw nothing except the harp‑players at the battle.'
segments.s310-g105: هوا (subject) + گشت (past of گشتن used as a copula 'became') + simile introduced by چون. Adjective نیلگون follows the noun چادر (typical Persian noun-adjective order). The clause reads literally 'the air became like an indigo tent.'
segments.s310-g106: زمین (subject) + شد (past/copular 'became') + به کردار (prepositional phrase 'in the manner/appearance of') + دریای خون (compound noun: دریای = 'sea of' + خون = 'blood'). The construction means 'the earth became like/as a sea of blood.' Note the poetic use of کردار for 'appearance/manner.'
segments.s310-g107: ز تیر ('from/through arrows' — ablative/cause) modifies آسمان شد ('the sky became'). چو introduces the simile 'like' and پر عقاب ('feather/wing of an eagle') is the image compared to the sky. The verb شد is the past/copular 'became.'
segments.s310-g108: نگه کرد is a compound past meaning 'fixed his gaze' (نگه as 'gaze' + کرد 'did' = 'he stared/kept his look'). خیره‌سر is an epithet/adjective ('headstrong') placed after the verb in apposition to the name that follows; افراسیاب is the named subject. Word order is poetic inversion: verb + epithet + name.
segments.s310-g109: بدید is past tense 'he saw' (subject implied from context). آن is the demonstrative 'that.' درفشان here functions as an adjectival epithet 'shining/glittering' modifying درفش 'banner,' and بنفش is the color 'purple.' The phrase means 'he saw that shining purple banner.'
segments.s310-g110: نهان کرد is past 'hid' (نهان 'hidden/secretly' + کرد 'did' = 'he hid [it]'). The following two بر phrases give the locative: بر قلبگه 'on/upon the bosom/heart' (i.e. tucked to his chest) and بر درفش repeats the locative relation to the banner; in context the object (the purple banner) is omitted here and understood from the previous clause. The doubled بر marks two overlapping locales; the sense is 'he concealed it at his bosom (with the banner).'
segments.s310-g111: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as the thing arranged. رده بر کشیده is a participial/compound adjectival phrase 'drawn up in ranks/lines' (رده = ranks, بر کشیده = 'drawn upon/arranged'). بماند (past/poetic) means 'remained' or 'he caused to remain,' so the clause conveys 'he left/kept the army drawn up in ranks.'
segments.s310-g112: خود و نامداران توران is a coordinated subject phrase 'he himself and Turan's famed/renowned men.' The verb براند (poetic past) means 'drove off' or 'put to flight.' The object 'them' (the enemy) is omitted and understood from context. Note the compact epic word order: subject phrase followed by the verb.
segments.s310-g153: ز چیزی که بود اندر آن رزمگاه is a prepositional/relative phrase meaning 'whatever there was in that battlefield.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; چیزی with the relative که بود ('that was') yields 'the thing(s) that were'; اندر آن رزمگاه is the locative 'in that battlefield' modifying چیزی.
segments.s310-g154: ببخشید is the past tense verb 'bestowed/gave' (بخشیدن) placed before its subject in poetic word order; شاه جهان is the subject ('the king of the world') and بر سپاه is the dative/benefactive phrase 'to/upon the army' (بر used here as 'to').
segments.s310-g155: وز آنجا is a compound sense 'and/from there' (classical poetic connective indicating sequence); بشد is the past/optative-like form 'it happened / he went / he became' with شاه as its subject following the verb (poetic inversion), and به بهشت گنگ means 'to the muted/silent paradise' (به = to; گنگ = 'mute, muffled').
segments.s310-g156: همه لشکر آباد is a nominal phrase 'the whole army [was] prosperous/settled' (آباد as predicate/adjective); با ساز جنگ is an adjunct 'with the instruments/gear of war' (با = with; ساز here = 'means/instrument(s)' used with جنگ 'war').
segments.s310-g157: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آگاهی آمد is the nominal + verb 'news/word arrived' (آمد past 'came'); به ماچین و چین marks the destinations 'to Maçin and Chin' (به = to).
segments.s310-g158: ز ترکان وز شاه ایران‌زمین is a coordinated genitive/agent phrase 'about/from the Turks and about/from the king of Iran'; the first ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', then ترکان 'the Turks'; وز here functions as 'and from' (classical compressed ו+ز), and شاه ایران‌زمین 'the king of the Iranian realm.'
segments.s310-g159: بپیچید is a past verb (from پیچیدن) 'were turned/struck/twisted' placed before the subject for poetic order; فغفور و خاقان are the coordinated subjects (Byzantine emperor and the khagan) and به درد is a prepositional phrase 'into/with pain' meaning 'in sorrow/afflicted by grief.'
segments.s310-g160: ز تخت مهی هر کسی یاد کرد is 'every person remembered the moon-like throne.' ز (از) here introduces the object of remembrance ('of/from the moon-throne'); تخت مهی is a compound 'throne (تخت) described as مهی, i.e. moon-like/beautiful'; هر کسی یاد کرد = 'each one recalled/remembered' (یاد + کرد forming the verb 'remembered').
segments.s310-g281: از آن (a prepositional phrase) gives the cause and precedes the clause: 'because of that'. شاد شد شاه is a simple past construction (شد = became) with the subject شاه after the verb for poetic word order, meaning 'the king became glad'. و links a second verb to the same subject. بنواختشان is past tense with the enclitic object -شان 'them' and means 'he caused them to play' (benavākhtan, poetic form for making music); the subject is the same شاه carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s310-g282: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one / each in turn' and modifies the verb that follows. به اندازه is a circumstantial phrase 'according to measure/degree' (به here marking manner: 'by/according to'). بنشاختشان is a past-tense verb with enclitic -شان 'them' from the rare/archaic verb بنشاختن meaning 'to appraise/recognize/assign a status'; the implied subject is the king from the previous clause.
segments.s310-g283: در introduces a locative phrase 'in/within'. گنجهای is the plural construct 'treasures/treasure-vaults' and is linked to the following noun نیا in a possessive/genitive relation: 'the treasuries of the ancestors'. برگشاد is a past participial/past-tense verb 'opened' (poetic compound form), with the subject implied from the narrative (the king).
segments.s310-g284: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from/concerning' introducing what follows as the content that was not recalled. پیوند و مهرش is a coordinated phrase 'the bond (kinship) and his affection/favour' — note مهرش uses the enclitic -ش 'his'. نکرد is the negated past 'did not do/ make', so together with ایچ یاد ('no/none remembrance') it yields 'he made no remembrance' (i.e. he did not remember). ایچ is an archaic emphatic negator meaning 'nothing/not at all', placed before یاد 'remembrance/trace' to stress absence.
segments.s310-g285: ز begins a partitive/listing phrase 'from' or 'out of'. دینار و دیبای گوهرنگار lists goods: 'dinar(s) and dība (brocade) adorned with jewels' — گوهرنگار is a compound adjective 'jeweled / gem‑encrusted' modifying دیبا (here in classical order following the noun).
segments.s310-g286: هیونان (pl.) 'the beasts/animals' is the subject. شایسته here functions adjectivally/adverbially as 'suitable/appropriate'. کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' and with بار (object) forms the light‑verb construction بار کردن 'to load' in past tense: 'they loaded (the beasts) with a suitable load.' Word order is typical classical Persian where adjectives or adverbial qualifiers can intervene.
segments.s310-g287: همیدون appears as a proper name (subject or actor in the narrative). ز marks source 'from', followed by گنج 'treasure' and درم 'dirham(s)'; the numeral صد هزار (one hundred thousand) quantifies the dirhams. The noun phrase order (source + coin + number) is a poetic/compact listing of what was taken from the vault.
segments.s310-g288: ببردند is the past plural 'they took / they carried away' (third plural), so the agents are plural (not necessarily the single name above). با is the instrumental preposition 'with/by', and آلت کارزار is a genitive/compound 'implements/tools of battle' (in later Persian 'آلتِ کارزار' = weapons/war‑gear). Together: 'they carried them off with instruments of war.'
segments.s310-g65: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed first for emphasis; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (3rd person) and introduces the reported clause; کز is an archaic fused form (که + از) meaning 'from' here and introduces the source phrase گردش آسمان 'the motion/rotation of the heavens' (گردش = motion/circuit, modified by the following noun آسمان).
segments.s310-g66: نیابد is the negative present/subjunctive form (ن + یابد) meaning 'does not/ cannot find or obtain' (3rd person); گذر here means 'passage/access' and takes دانشی as its object in an abstract sense ('access to knowledge'); بی‌گمان is a sentence-final adverb 'undoubtedly' that strengthens the negation.
segments.s310-g67: چو is the conditional 'if/when' introducing the clause; دشمن is the subject 'the enemy'; همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually' or 'so'; جان is the direct object 'life(s)'; بسیچد is an archaic/uncertain verb form (here functioning as the verb meaning roughly 'gathers/slays/takes'); نه چیز is a negated nominal phrase 'no thing / nothing' that states there will be nothing else.
segments.s310-g68: بکوشیم is a 1st-person-plural optative/subjunctive ('let us strive' / 'we must strive') expressing resolve; ناچار is an adverb/adjective 'necessarily/inevitably' modifying the verb; یک دست literally 'one hand' (here 'even with a single hand' — i.e. with limited means); نیز 'also/still' adds the sense of 'even so'.
segments.s310-g69: اگر is the conditional 'if'; the repeated سر به سر is an idiomatic intensifier (literally 'head by head' → 'one and all' / 'completely'); تن به کشتن is an infinitival phrase 'body to killing' = 'the body to be killed'; دهیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'we give' (here 'we should/would give' or 'we give up'), completing the conditional clause.
segments.s310-g70: وگر ('and/or if' — an alternative conditional) introduces the second possibility; ایرجی is the proper name 'Iraj' in the oblique/accusative-like form; تاج بر سر نهیم is a verbal phrase where نهیم (from نهاد) is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive 'we place/put' and بر سر 'upon the head' marks the physical placement of the تاج (crown).
segments.s310-g71: برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'; خروش is the noun 'cry/roar' as the subject/what issued forth; از دو پرده‌سرای is a prepositional phrase 'from the two پرده‌سرا' (پرده‌سرا is a compound meaning 'curtain-house/theatre/tent' — poetically 'the two curtained halls/tents'). Word order (verb before subject) is poetic inversion.
segments.s310-g72: جهان is the subject 'the world'; پر شد is the two-word predicate 'became full / was filled' (پر = full of, شد = became); از ناله کرنای is a prepositional phrase 'with the lament/cry of the trumpet' where کرنای is the genitive/attributive form of کرنا (trumpet), so the world is filled with trumpet-laments.
segments.s310-g177: به پوزش is a prepositional phrase marking manner/purpose ('with/as an apology') that modifies the verb فرستاد (3rd singular past 'he sent'). نزدیک شاه is an adverbial/directional phrase ('toward/into the king's presence' or 'before the king') following the verb; the subject is omitted (third person). The simple past verb فرستاد carries the main action and takes these modifiers.
segments.s310-g178: فرستادگان is the plural subject 'the envoys'. برگرفتند is a 3rd-person-plural past tense verb (برداشتن/برگرفتن) meaning 'they took up' or here idiomatically 'they set out/took (the) road'; راه is its direct object 'the road/path'. Word order (verb before object) is literary but the verb still governs راه.
segments.s310-g179: بزرگان چین is a noun phrase 'the great ones/nobles of China' (چین modifies بزرگان). بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'immediately/at once' that follows the subject, and آمدند is the 3rd-person-plural past verb 'they came'. The clause is a straightforward subject–adverb–verb order.
segments.s310-g180: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase 'in/after one week' (به marks the time span), از چین marks origin 'from China', and به گنگ آمدند is the directional clause 'they came to the Ganga' (به as directional 'to', آمدند 3pl past). Note that the same preposition به appears twice with different senses (temporal vs. directional).
segments.s310-g181: جهاندار پیروز is a titled subject 'the victorious world-ruler' (noun + epithet). بنواختشان is a past-tense form of نواختن with the object clitic -شان 'for them' — here meaning 'he caused music to be played for them' or 'he played for them'. The prefixed بـ is a literary marker on the past stem.
segments.s310-g182: چنانچون is a comparative/phrasing particle 'just as/as', and ببایست is an impersonal/past-form 'it was fitting/necessary' (classical usage 'ببایست' = 'befitted'). بنشاختشان is a 3rd-singular past verb with the clitic -شان 'them' meaning here 'he received/recognized them'. Together the clause means 'and he received/recognized them as was fitting.'
segments.s310-g183: بپذرفت is a 3rd-person-singular past 'he accepted/received'. چیزی که آورده بود is a noun with a relative clause: چیزی 'a thing/something', که introduces 'that', آورده is the past participle 'brought' and بود is the past auxiliary 'had been/was' — together 'the thing that had been brought'.
segments.s310-g184: طرایف is the subject/predicate 'trifles/curiosities'. The following list 'بد و بدره و پرده' enumerates qualities/items (بد 'bad/mean', بدره a poetic noun often 'beauty/full-moon-like gem', and پرده 'curtain/veil'), and بود is the copular past 'were'. The whole clause classifies what had been brought as mere trifles of those kinds.
segments.s310-g81: کسی را نبد = a classical/poetic negation: نبد (نبود) means 'there was not' and with کسی را yields 'there was no one.' را here is the object/indefiniteness marker common in older Persian constructions. بر تن خویش مهر = a prepositional phrase 'a seal/mark upon his own body': بر + تن (body) with خویش ('his own', here formed from خوی + enclitic ش) and مهر as the noun 'seal/mark' placed after the phrase.
segments.s310-g82: به قیر اندر اندود is a locative/coating phrase 'coated/smeared in pitch' (به + قیر + اندر + اندود). سپهر follows and names the scene/agent 'the sky'; گفتی here does not match the supplied noun-senses (the text uses a past-form meaning 'said/was said' or functions as part of the poetic statement). The whole segment describes the sky as being coated with tar/pitch.
segments.s310-g83: همانگه is an adverb 'at that moment' placed before the verb; برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth.' یکی تیره باد = 'a certain dark wind' where یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one' modifying the noun تیره باد ('dark/ominous wind'). Word order is adverb + verb + indefinite + noun, typical of epic diction.
segments.s310-g84: که introduces a relative clause 'that/which.' هرگز ندارد کسی آن به یاد = 'no one ever has that in memory' (literally: 'never has anyone that to memory') — ندارد is present/past-habitual 'has' in the sense 'is possessed/remembered', and به یاد is the idiom 'in memory/remembered.' The clause modifies the preceding 'dark wind.'
segments.s310-g85: همی is a continuous/progressive particle here meaning 'kept on' or simply marking the action; خاک برداشت is a compound verbal idea 'lifted/raised dust' (برداشت = took up/removed). از رزمگاه is the source phrase 'from the battlefield.' The verb is past and the construction describes a repeated or vivid action.
segments.s310-g86: بزد is the past verb 'it struck' (subject implied = the wind/dust). بر سر و چشم = a double prepositional object 'upon the head and (upon the) eye(s)'; توران سپاه = 'the Turanian host/army' (noun phrase in the oblique position). The clause therefore means the dust/wind hit the heads and eyes of the Turanian army.
segments.s310-g87: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; سرها is the plural 'heads.' همی again marks the continuing action; ترگها (terg/hag) are 'rags' or 'shreds,' and برگرفت is a compound past verb 'took off/removed' (literally 'lifted up'). The phrase describes rags being taken from heads (i.e., garments/shrouds torn off).
segments.s310-g88: بماند is past 'remained/was left'; اندر آن is 'in/at that'; شاه ترکان = 'the king of the Turks'; شگفت here indicates astonishment/wonder. Together: 'that remained in the Turk king as astonishment' = 'that left the Turk king astonished.' Word order places the verb first for emphasis.
segments.s310-g49: به کسره + noun phrase marks direction/placement: به کنده در افتاد is a compact clause meaning “fell into the dug (hole).” به here expresses motion/toward (classical usage), کنده modifies در (a dug/dug-out place), and افتاد is the past-tense verb “fell” placed after the phrase. چندی سوار is attached as its subject: چندی = several; سوار = rider/horseman.
segments.s310-g50: بپیچید is a past-tense verb (بـ- prefix + پیچید) meaning “turned” or “twisted” and governs the following phrase. دیگر is an adjective/pronoun meaning “other/another,” and سر here means “head” (literal head/part) used idiomatically with از کارزار to mean turning away from the fight. The construction places the verb before its complements in poetic narrative.
segments.s310-g51: ز یک دست functions as an instrumental/agent phrase “with one hand” (ز here = ‘by/with’ in poetic usage). رستم is the subject, and برآمد (past tense) means “rose/appeared/issued forth.” The clause ends with ز دشت specifying source/place (“from the plain”), so the verb bridges the instrumental and locative phrases.
segments.s310-g52: This clause gives the resulting scene: ز + noun indicates cause/origin (ز گرد سواران = ‘from the dust of the horsemen’). هوا is the subject (“the air”), تیره is the predicate adjective “dark,” and گشت (past of گشتن) means “became.” Word order places the subject before the copular verb and the cause phrase after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s310-g53: ز دست دگر is a prepositional phrase meaning “from the other hand/side” (z = from; دست = hand/side; دگر = other). The three proper names are listed in apposition and coordinated by و. In narrative Persian such a phrase often introduces the agents who arrive or act from an alternate direction.
segments.s310-g54: به پیش اندرون is a locative phrase meaning “before/in the presence of the inner quarters” (به پیش = to/in the presence of; اندرون = inner place). ناله names the sound (“lament/cry”), and بوق و کوس are the sounding instruments (horns and war-drums); the sequence gives the scene: the horns and drums issue a wailing sound toward the inner court.
segments.s310-g55: شهنشاه is the subject “the king/emperor.” با + noun (با کاویانی درفش) is an accompaniment/instrumental phrase meaning “with the Kayanian banner/standard.” The phrase places the royal figure visually beside his standard in the scene.
segments.s310-g56: This clause states the resulting color of the air: هوا شد = “the air became,” with شد as the past copular/verb “became.” ز تیغ سواران gives the cause/agent (“from the swords of the horsemen”), and بنفش is the predicate adjective “purple.” Word order places the cause phrase after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s310-g41: بر آن تاختن is a prepositional phrase + verbal noun: بر (on/upon) + آن (that) + تاختن (the charging, here a verbal noun meaning 'the charge'). جنبش و ساز are two nouns ('movement' and 'music/tune') listed as the things absent; the negation is expressed by the final نه (classical elliptical negator) rather than a separate verb. Word order places the locative/temporal phrase before the items being negated.
segments.s310-g42: همان here (= 'the same' / 'that very') modifies ناله (a cry/wail). The phrase lists instruments/voices (بوق 'horn/trumpet' and آواز 'call/voice') and closes with نه to continue the 'neither...nor' construction begun in the previous segment; the order is determiner → noun → modifiers → final negative particle.
segments.s310-g43: چو رفتند is a temporal clause ('when they went / when they came'); نزدیک functions as an adverb ('near / close') following the verb. پرده سرای is a compound (curtain of the house/pavilion); note that the possessed sense of سرای ('house, pavilion') is not in the provided senses list, so its sense is flagged below as unknown.
segments.s310-g44: برآمد is a past verb meaning 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن is a verbal noun 'the roaring/crying' and here functions as the subject/theme of برآمد; کرنای (trumpet) names the source of that cry. The order is verb (arose) + verbal noun + instrument, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s310-g45: This short clause strings nominal phrases and a verb: غو طبل = 'the uproar/clamor of the drum' (noun + apposition/possession), بر کوهه = 'on the hill' (preposition + place), then زین بخاست = 'he rose from the saddle' (زین = 'saddle' followed by بخاست 'rose/rose up'). The subject is omitted (typical in epic narrative) and is understood from context.
segments.s310-g46: درفش سیه را is the direct object phrase ('the black standard' with را as the definite/direct-object marker). برآورد is a past verb here meaning 'he lifted/raised' (literal sense 'brought up'); راست modifies the manner/direction ('straight' / 'directly'). Word order: object + verb + directional adverb.
segments.s310-g47: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the army/host'. هر آن کس که is a relative construction 'every one who...'. بد پیشرو is a modifier of کس (an epithet for those in front); بد is the adjective 'bad/ill' modifying پیشرو ('vanguard/foremost'), i.e. a critical or negative qualifier attached to 'the vanguard'.
segments.s310-g48: برانگیختند is a past transitive verb 'they aroused/roused' (here 'they stirred/spurred [their] horses'), with اسب its object. The coordinating و links the next verb برخاست 'rose/there arose', and غو is the noun 'uproar/tumult' that results; the clause thus presents two linked results: horses were stirred and a clamor rose.
segments.s310-g273: ز introduces a source/reasonal prepositional phrase ('from/owing to'); کاری is the noun 'act/deed' modified by the relative clause که سازد ('that he/one makes/does'). همی is an archaic continuous/modal particle that gives a present/future sense to سازد. برخورد is a noun 'encounter/impact/consequence' that completes the idea: 'from the deed that he does, an encounter (or consequence) (will) arise.'
segments.s310-g274: بدین ('in this way/thus') is built from ب + دین and functions adverbially; آمد is a past/result verb 'came/occurred' (used here as 'resulted/appeared'); و connects the clauses; هم adds the sense 'also/likewise'; ب + دین again form بدین 'thus'; بگذرد is a present/subjunctive or future-marked stem 'passes/will pass'. Together: 'it came thus, and likewise it will pass thus.'
segments.s310-g275: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is a past-tense verb 'heard' whose subject is لشکر ('the army'), though word order places the noun before the verb; ز رستم سخن is a prepositional phrase 'a word/speech from Rostam' (ز = 'from', سخن = 'word/speech').
segments.s310-g276: یکی ('one/a single') modifies پاسخ ('reply/answer'); نو is an adjective 'new/fresh' that also modifies پاسخ; فگندند is the archaic past 3rd-person-plural 'they threw/they cast' used figuratively as 'they uttered/they cast forth' the reply; بن here is an archaic/postpositive particle (placed after the verb) functioning adverbially — likely 'thereupon' or a discourse particle. The clause means 'they cast forth a single fresh reply.'
segments.s310-g277: که introduces reported content ('that'); ما is the subject 'we'; سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'completely/utterly' and modifies the predicate; شاه را is the marked indirect object 'to the king' (را marks definiteness/emphasis); بنده‌ایم is present plural copular/noun-predicate 'we are servants.' The whole: 'that we are completely the king's servants.'
segments.s310-g278: ابا here appears as the noun 'reluctance/refusal' (or an expression of reluctance) and stands with بندگی ('servitude') to form the object/topic phrase; دوست دارنده‌ایم is a compound present form meaning 'we are lovers/fond (of) / we hold (affection for)'. Grammatically, the object(s) (ابا بندگی) appear before the predicate دوست دارنده‌ایم: 'we are fond/are holders of servitude (i.e. we do not shrink from serving).'
segments.s310-g279: به خشکی ('on the dry land') and بر آب ('on the water') are coordinated locative phrases (به ... و بر ...). فرمان is the nominal predicate 'command/rule/order' and وراست (وراست) is an adjectival predicate 'upright/right/just' agreeing in sense with فرمان; the copula is omitted (typical in classical Persian), so 'the command is just on land and on water.'
segments.s310-g280: همه 'all' quantifies the subject of کهترانیم ('we are lesser/inferior'); that clause states 'we are all inferior.' The conjunction و links a second nominal clause 'پیمان وراست' in which پیمان ('the pledge/treaty') takes the predicate وراست ('is right/true'). Again the copula is omitted: 'and the pledge is sound.'
segments.s310-g113: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; the whole phrase is a compact Persian noun phrase where the numeral traditionally follows the noun (هزار is placed after مردی in the line). شایسته is an adjective ‘worthy’ qualifying the men, and مردی here functions as 'men' (mardī) so the sense is 'a thousand worthy men (of his kin)'. Word order is literary: 'from relatives (خویشان) a thousand worthy men'.
segments.s310-g114: به نزدیک is a locative/directional phrase 'close to / near'; او is the third-person pronoun 'him'; بود is the past-tense copula 'was'; در کارزار is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the battle'. The verb (بود) comes after the locative phrase, a normal poetic word order: 'was near him in the battlefield.'
segments.s310-g115: به marks direction 'into/by way of'; بیراه here means 'off the beaten track / astray' (a detour); راه بیابان is the noun phrase 'desert road' or 'wilderness path'; گرفت is the simple past 'took' (he took that route). The sequence expresses motion: 'he took a wrong/desert path.'
segments.s310-g116: به رنج تن is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by the toil/suffering of his body' (به here = 'by/with'); از دشمنان marks the source or object 'from the enemies'; جان گرفت is a compound verbal idea 'he took life' (i.e. slew enemies), with گرفت as the past-tense verb. The phrase states the means and the result: through bodily toil he took lives from the foes.
segments.s310-g117: ز لشکر marks 'from the army/host'; نیا را has the object marker را attached to نیا, indicating the king is searching for that person/name (Niyâ) — the exact lexical sense here is a proper-name usage not covered by the provided senses; همی preceding the verb gives a continual/progressive sense 'kept/was'; جست is the past of 'seek/search'; شاه at the end is the subject. Literal order: 'From the army Niyâ the king was searching.'
segments.s310-g118: بیامد is the past 'he came'; دمان is a locative noun 'skirt/edge' (here: the flank or skirt of the formation); تا is 'until/up to'; به قلب سپاه is the destination 'to the heart (center) of the army'. The phrase describes motion inward: 'he came to the skirts and as far as the army's core.'
segments.s310-g119: ز هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every direction'; پویید is past 'he searched / sought' or 'he moved about searching'; و links clauses; چندی shoud be read here as 'for a while'; شتافت is past 'he hastened' or 'rushed'. The structure is two coordinated actions: he searched all around and then hurried on for a time.
segments.s310-g120: نشان پی is 'a trace/mark' (نشان) with پی indicating following/trace (together: 'sign/trace'); شاه توران is a genitive-like juxtaposition 'the king of Turan'; نیافت is the past negative 'did not find' (ن- + یافت). The clause means he did not find any sign of the Turanian king.
segments.s310-g121: سپه is the subject 'the host/army'. چون here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. نگه کرد is a two-part past expression: the noun نگه 'gaze' with the light verb کرد 'did/made' to mean 'looked'. در قلبگاه is an adverbial phrase 'into/at the heart/center' placed after the verb as the locative complement.
segments.s310-g122: ندیدند is past plural with the negative prefix ن‑ + دیدند 'they saw', so 'they did not see'. جایی here means 'any place/any trace' (i.e. 'any sign of'); درفش سیاه is the noun phrase 'the black standard/banner' (درفش = standard, سیاه = black) serving as the object/thing of which no trace was seen. Note the verb precedes the object phrase, a normal Persian order.
segments.s310-g123: ز is the poetic form of از, here 'from' (i.e. 'to the king' / 'they asked of the king'). شه (شاه) is 'the king'. خواستند is past plural 'they requested/asked' and frames the reported appeal; آن زمان 'at that time' gives the temporal setting. زینهار is an interjection meaning 'beware!' or 'take heed!' and functions as the content of the plea (reported admonition) placed after the verb.
segments.s310-g124: فروریختند is past plural 'they collapsed/they fell apart' (here describing the breakdown or fall of martial apparatus). آلت کارزار is a genitive construction 'instruments/implements of war' (آلت 'implements', modified by کارزار 'of battle'), serving as the subject/thing that 'collapsed'. The verb therefore refers back to these war implements rather than the people.
segments.s310-g125: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خسرو is the subject proper name 'Khosrow'. چنان 'so/that' refers back to the preceding situation. دید is the past 'he saw'. بنواختشان is a past transitive verb with the object suffix ‑شان 'them' from نواختن: here it means 'he struck/punished them' (not musical playing), the verb + pronoun forming a single word in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s310-g126: ز لشکر جدا is a prepositional phrase 'apart from the army' (ز = from/of, لشکر = army, جدا = apart). جایگه ساختشان is a verb phrase: جایگه 'place/position' + ساخت (past 'made/appointed') with the object suffix ‑شان 'them' — 'he made/assigned them a place'. Word order places the locative/comparative phrase before the verb.
segments.s310-g127: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he ordered/commanded' (literally 'he said' used as a command). تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause 'so that/that', and تخت زرین نهند is that subordinate clause: تخت زرین 'a golden throne' (noun + adjective) and نهند is the subjunctive/imperative-form verb 'they should place/let be placed' (subject implied or general), used after a command to indicate what is to be done.
segments.s310-g128: به خیمه در gives the locative 'into/in the tent' (به = to/into, خیمه = tent, در = in/inside — poetic word order places both prepositions/locatives). آرایش چین is a noun phrase 'arrangement/ornament of folds' (آرایش = arrangement/ornament, چین = folds), and نهند repeats the subjunctive/optative 'they should place' matching the command structure begun by تا; the object precedes the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s310-g305: نواها is a plural substantive here (literally “sounds/notes” in its basic lexeme) used at the start of the line; که introduces a relative clause (“that / who”). از شهرها is a simple prepositional phrase “from the cities,” and یادگار is a noun meaning “memento” or “spoils,” functioning as the object or qualifier of what was taken. Word order is poetic (head noun then relative clause and prepositional phrase).
segments.s310-g306: گروگان is the direct object “hostage(s)” and ستد is a past tense verb “took/seized.” The phrase ترک چینی هزار places ethnic labels (Turk, Chinese) before the numeral; in epic Persian numerals may follow the counted noun, so this reads “a thousand Turks and Chinese.” No explicit subject is given in the line (it is carried from the surrounding narrative).
segments.s310-g307: سپرد is a past-tense verb “he entrusted/handed over.” آن زمان is a temporal adverbial “at that time.” گیو را is the definite object marked by را (“Giv” as the person entrusted), and شهریار is an object-complement/title meaning “king” — together: “he entrusted Giv as king.” Note the verb appears early (poetic word order) and را marks the definite object.
segments.s310-g308: گزین کرد is a two-part past tense meaning “chose/selected” (گزین = choose + کرد = did/made). ز ایرانیان is a poetic form of از ایرانیان, “from the Iranians,” and ده هزار is the quantity “ten thousand” (numeral placed after the group in this poetic phrasing). The overall sense: “he selected ten thousand from the Iranians.”
segments.s310-g309: بدو is the prepositional/indirect-object form “to him.” گفت here functions as the speech-verb “he said” (the provided noun senses do not include the verbal use). کای is a vocative particle “O” addressing someone, مرد فرخنده is a descriptive vocative phrase “fortunate man/fortunate warrior,” and پی means “after/in pursuit of” (here instructing to follow or pursue). The sequence marks reported speech directed to that man.
segments.s310-g310: برو is an imperative “go.” با سپه is a prepositional phrase “with the army.” پیش here means “before / in the presence of” (i.e. bring or go into Kavus’s presence). کاووس is the proper name “Kavus.” کی at the end is an interrogative/emphatic particle in some contexts, but here functions as a final emphatic/colloquial particle attached to the command (its exact force is idiomatic in epic usage).
segments.s310-g193: که here introduces the clause (a wish/optative tone—'that/may you be...'). The two prepositions از and poetic ز both mark separation ('away from'); از من = 'away from me', then ز چین و ختن lists places ('from China and Khotan'). The predication دور باش is an imperative/optative phrase 'be distant' with the predicate at the end (typical classical/poetic order).
segments.s310-g194: ز here functions as 'because of / from' and introduces the reason clause. بد کردن خویش is a verbal noun phrase 'doing bad (behaviour) of oneself' (کردن = 'to do' used as main verb/noun here; خوی + ش = 'one's disposition/one's own'), and the clause ends with رنجور باش, an optative/imperative 'be afflicted' (باش as the predicative verb).
segments.s310-g195: هر آن کس که ... is the stock relative/indefinite 'whoever/anyone who'. The pronoun او is an emphatic/redundant subject in classical style. گم کند is a present/gnomic verbal form 'loses' (کند used as the present-tense auxiliary/marker), and راه خویش 'his way' is the object (راه = path; خوی + ش = 'his'). Word order places subject + relative + verb + object in a typical poetic sequence.
segments.s310-g196: بد آید is a short predicate 'bad comes / ill happens' (آید = 'happen' here). The following phrase بداندیش را کار پیش is object/topic marked by را: 'the affair (کار) of the ill‑wisher (بداندیش) (پیش = in prospect/forwards)'. Poetic word order places the predicate before the topical object (i.e. 'ill happens — the malicious man's affair (پیش)').
segments.s310-g197: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard'. The poetic word order places the verb close to چو and the subject (افراسیاب) follows; این سخن is the object 'these words/this speech'.
segments.s310-g198: پشیمان شد is a compound predication: پشیمان (regretful) + شد (became) = 'became regretful'. از introduces the cause or object of regret; کرده‌های کهن 'his old/ancient deeds' (کرده‌های = deeds, plural marker -های attached) is the noun phrase governed by از.
segments.s310-g199: This line strings two short clauses. بیفگند is a past action 'threw/cast down'. نام مهی is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the name/reputation of the moon‑like one' (مهی modifying نام). جان گرفت is a terse clause 'life was taken / the soul seized' (گرفت as passive/resultative here). The clauses are juxtaposed for dramatic effect.
segments.s310-g200: به بیراه is an adverbial phrase 'into/onto the wrong/astray way' (به + بیراه). راه بیابان گرفت is a nominal phrase 'he took the path of the desert' with گرفت as the past-tense verb 'took'. The repetition of گرفت and the inverted order (adverbial first) is a poetic device meaning 'he went astray and entered the wilderness road.'
