segments.s310-g225: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “when.” روشن شود is a verb phrase where روشن (adjective “bright”) + شود (present/subjunctive of شدن) yields “becomes bright.” تیره‌گون is a compound adjective (تیره + گون) meaning “dark-colored,” and اخترم is اختر + the possessive suffix -م “my,” so the whole phrase = “when my dark-colored star becomes bright.”
segments.s310-g226: This is a compact movement clause. به کشتی is the means/instrumental (“by/with a ship”), بر آب places the motion ‘on the water,’ زره qualifies the manner (armored / wearing armor), and بگذرم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive used for future action (“I will pass/go”), so together: “I will (armored) cross/ride by ship on the water.”
segments.s310-g227: ز here means “from” (source/target). دشمن is the noun ‘enemy.’ بخواهم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of خواستن used to express future intent (“I will seek/ask/exact”). همان is a demonstrative “that same,” and کین خویش = کین + خوی + ش is ‘my/one’s own vengeance’ (خوی + ش functions like a possessive), so the clause = “I will exact from the enemy that very vengeance of mine.”
segments.s310-g228: درفشان is used adjectivally/verbally as ‘resplendent / shining’ (literally ‘flag‑waving’), کنم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن “I will make/do,” and راه و آیین خویش is the direct object ‘my way and my custom.’ So grammatically it’s ‘(I) will make my way and custom shine/stand out.’
segments.s310-g229: چو again = “when.” کیخسرو is the subject (proper name). آگاه شد is a past tense/perfect construction (‘became aware’), where آگاه is adjective/noun and شد is the past tense marker of شدن. ز این سخن marks the source (‘from/through this speech’).
segments.s310-g230: که introduces the content clause (‘that …’). کار نو آورد is lit. ‘brought a new matter’ (آورد is past “brought”), and مرد کهن is the agent ‘the old man’ placed after the verb for stylistic emphasis (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian).
segments.s310-g231: به رستم marks the indirect object/target ‘to Rostam.’ چنین گفت is an inverted reporting phrase ‘thus (he) said,’ with گفت functioning as the verb ‘said,’ and کافراسیاب is the speaker (subject) named after the verb for poetic word‑order.
segments.s310-g232: سوی indicates direction ‘toward.’ گنگ دژ is a toponymic noun phrase ‘the Gank (or “Gong”) fortress.’ شد here functions as a verb meaning ‘went/moved/turned’ (classical usage of شدن for motion/occurrence), and ز دریای آب is a prepositional phrase ‘from the sea (of) water’ giving origin. Word order is poetic: direction + place + verb + origin.
segments.s310-g1: This is an adverbial/verb phrase placed up front in the old epic order: 'به ره' (‘toward/on the way’) gives the directional frame, followed by the participial/past form 'کنده' (from کندن, 'to cut/clear') meaning 'having cut/cleared a way'. 'پیش و پس' ('before and after') specifies location of the cutting, and 'اندر سپاه' ('in/among the host') names where. In context this whole segment describes the act of clearing a path through the army.
segments.s310-g2: 'پس' is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'after that'. 'کنده' repeats the verbal idea (past/participial use of کندن) — here continuing the narrative: 'he then (having) cut/moved'. 'با لشکر و پیل شاه' is a simple accompaniment phrase 'with the army and the king's elephants' (با = with). Together the clause says he departed/moved accompanied by the troops and royal elephants.
segments.s310-g3: 'سپهدار ترکان' is the subject phrase 'the commander of the Turks'. 'چو' here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'شب در شکست' places the time/location: 'night during the rout/defeat' (در + شکست = 'in/at the rout'). This whole segment sets the time and subject: the Turkish commander, when night came amid the rout.
segments.s310-g4: 'میان' ('amid, in the midst') gives locative sense; 'با سپه تاختن' is a verbal noun/infinitive construction 'the charging with the host' (تاختن = to charge). The object-marker 'را' marks that infinitive as the direct object of 'ببست' (past 'he stopped/closed'). So syntactically: 'amid (the fighting) he stopped the host's charge.'
segments.s310-g5: 'ز لشکر' ('from the army') marks source. 'جهاندیدگان' is a nominal compound meaning experienced men/veterans; 'را' is the definite/object marker and 'بخواند' is the past-tense verb 'he summoned/called'. Word order (source–object–verb) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s310-g6: The second clause begins again with 'ز' indicating source/relating to 'کار گذشته' ('the deed/affair gone by' = the earlier action). 'فراوان' qualifies 'many' or 'in great number' and 'براند' (from راندن) is a past narrative verb 'he drove (away/sent)'. The clause says he drove away/sent off many in relation to the previous action.
segments.s310-g7: 'چنین گفت' is a formulaic narrative clause 'thus he said'. The following 'کاین شوم پر کیمیا' is reported content introduced by کاین ('this/that which'); 'شوم' here is adjectival 'ill‑omened' rather than the first‑person verb, 'پر' means 'full (of)', and 'کیمیا' (alchemy) functions figuratively (elixir/secret/power). The segment reports his spoken judgment about somebody or something as 'ill‑omened and full of (strange) alchemy'.
segments.s310-g8: Again 'چنین' ('thus/so') links to the previous manner. 'خیره شد' is a perfective/past 'he became fixed/stared' (شد as copula/auxiliary with خیره 'fixed-gazing/astonished'). 'بر سپاه' ('upon the host') is the prepositional object; 'نیا' here follows 'سپاه' (likely a name or epithet attached to the host). The clause says he stood staring at that host.
segments.s310-g257: که is the relative/complementizer 'that/for'; دریای is the genitive/construct form 'the sea' (with poetic -ی); با موج 'with waves' links the sea to its characteristic; چندین سپاه 'many a host' is an apposition to دریای, enumerating forces or multitudes associated with the sea. Word order is typical Persian: noun + modifiers.
segments.s310-g258: سر و کار is an idiom meaning 'dealings/affairs' or 'to be engaged with' (the two nouns together yield the meaning); با باد 'with the wind' shows the thing the sea/hosts must deal with; و شش ماه راه 'and a road/journey of six months' is an additional object of سر و کار. The phrase as a whole says what they must contend with.
segments.s310-g259: که داند functions as the rhetorical 'who knows' (که = who/that; داند = knows, 3sg present of دانستن). The following clause کی/که آید ز آب 'who will come out of the water' is introduced by a second که; بیرون 'out, outside' modifies آید. Multiple که's are typical in classical Persian to introduce nested clauses.
segments.s310-g260: بد آمد is a verbal phrase 'badly came' meaning 'it turned out ill' or 'ill befell'; سپه را has را marking the affected party (the host/army), and ز افراسیاب gives the source/cause ('because of/from Afrasiab'). The clause therefore states that misfortune came upon the army from Afrasiab.
segments.s310-g261: چو = 'when' (temporal). خشکی بود = 'there was dryness' i.e. 'it was dry land'; ما (we) is the subject of the following predication; به جنگ اندریم literally 'we are/stand in war' — اندریم (from اندر used as a stative/locative) expresses that 'we were in/engaged in war'. The tense/aspect is narrative past.
segments.s310-g262: به دریا = 'to/into the sea' (i.e. when at sea); به کام نهنگ is an idiom meaning 'to the whale's desire' or 'at the mercy of the whale' (کام here rendered as appetite/desire); اندریم repeats the locative/stative 'we are/we fell into'. The contrast shows land-battle vs. being vulnerable at sea.
segments.s310-g263: همی گفت is a habitual/continuative verbal phrase meaning 'he was saying / kept saying' (همی marks continuity). هر گونه‌ای 'in every sort/way' qualifies هر کسی 'each person' — word order places manner before the person. This is a generalizing statement about the variety of voices.
segments.s310-g264: بدانگه 'thereupon' (a temporal adverb) introduces the result clause; که is the complementizer; گفتارها شد بسی literally 'the speeches became many/abundant' (شد = became, بسی = much/enough) — the clause reports that many speeches followed.
segments.s310-g249: This is a simple past clause with the 2nd-person plural subject pronoun شما first, the object رنج بسیار (a noun phrase: رنج 'suffering' + adverbial intensifier بسیار 'greatly') and the past verb برداشتید at the end. In classical Persian the compound رنج برداشتن = 'to endure suffering' is used; برداشتید is the past 2pl form. Word order is Subject — Object — Verb (typical Persian).
segments.s310-g250: بر و بوم is an idiomatic pair meaning 'land / dwelling / homeland' (here treated as a unit), with آباد postpositive (follows the noun) qualifying it as 'prosperous/inhabited'. The verb بگذاشتید (past, 2pl, prefixed b- as in poetic usage) means 'you left / you placed / you caused to remain' — here 'you left (it) in a prosperous state'. و is the coordinating 'and'.
segments.s310-g251: همین ('this same') modifies رنج. بر خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'upon yourselves' (بر = 'on/upon', خویشتن = 'yourself/selves'). برنهید is a jussive/imperative-type verb from بر نهادن 'to put/assume'; here it functions as an instruction: 'assume/put upon yourselves'. The clause orders the addressees to take on the hardship.
segments.s310-g252: از آن به که is a classical comparative/concessive formula meaning 'rather than / instead of that' (literally 'from that to that'). گیتی 'world' is the object of the verb دهید (present/jussive 2pl 'you give' / 'you hand over'), with به دشمن 'to the enemy' marking the recipient. The verb is final as usual.
segments.s310-g253: بماند is a jussive/optative 3rd-person verb meaning 'may it remain' or 'let it remain'. ز ما 'of/from us' marks possession (poetic ז for از), and نام is 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame. تا introduces a temporal limit 'until' and رستخیز is the noun 'resurrection / uprising'. The clause expresses a wish for lasting fame.
segments.s310-g254: This is a short follow-on phrase describing the state in which the name endures: به پیروزی 'in/with victory' (به marking state or direction), و coordinating, دشمن اندر گریز 'the enemy in flight' with اندر = 'in/within' (archaic) and گریز 'flight/escape'. The order places the circumstance after the initial clause.
segments.s310-g255: شدند is the simple past 3rd-person-plural 'they became'. اندر آن پهلوانان places them 'among those champions' or 'there, those champions', and دژم is an adjective 'grim/sullen' that follows the noun phrase — a common poetic order. Overall: 'They became grim among those champions.'
segments.s310-g256: This is a compressed descriptive string: دهان پر ز باد 'mouth(s) full of wind' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', باد = 'wind' — here a poetic way to suggest heavy breathing), ابروان پر زخم 'eyebrows/brows full of wounds' (i.e. brows scarred or wounded). Adjectival/partitive phrases are stacked for vivid imagery.
segments.s310-g9: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' placed at the clause start. جمله ایرانیان is a quantified noun phrase: جمله 'all/entire' + ایرانیان 'the Iranians'. خفته‌اند is a past-participle + auxiliary (خفته + اند) used resultatively: 'are asleep' / 'have slept' (third-person plural agreement).
segments.s310-g10: همه لشکر ما = 'the whole of our army' (همه before لشکر marks totality, ما is the possessor). برآشفته‌اند is a compound participle (prefix بر- + آشفته) + اند, describing a state/result: 'have been thrown into disorder' or 'are in confusion' (third-person plural).
segments.s310-g11: کنون ما sets time + subject 'now we'. ز دل بیم بیرون کنیم is a classical word order with the preposition ز 'from/out of' + دل 'heart' + بیم 'fear' followed by the verb کنيم (first-person-plural). کنيم here is hortative/subjunctive ('let us / we will cause/remove'), and بیرون is a directional adverb 'out'.
segments.s310-g12: سحرگه is a fronted temporal adverb 'at dawn'. بر ایشان شبیخون کنیم places the target (بر ایشان 'against/on them') before the noun شبیخون 'surprise attack' and the verb کنيم (hortative/subjunctive 'let us/let's do it'); Persian allows object/oblique phrase before the verb in this style.
segments.s310-g13: گر introduces a conditional ('if'). امشب is the time adverb 'tonight'. بر ایشان is the oblique 'upon/against them'. بیابیم is first-person-plural present/subjunctive ('we find / we can get'), used here in a protasis 'if we can seize/get hold of them'. دست is used idiomatically for 'a hold/power' (i.e. getting control), not the literal hand.
segments.s310-g14: به بیشی ابر تخت is a prepositional/nominal phrase placed before the modal. به here introduces the location/seat ('on/upon' in classical use); بیشی + ابر تخت is a poetic compounded description ('the greater/lofty cloud-throne'). باید + نشست expresses necessity: باید followed by the verbal noun/infinitive (نشست 'to sit/take a seat') = 'one must sit / it will be necessary to sit'.
segments.s310-g15: وگر 'and if/but if' again introduces a conditional. بختمان in context functions as 'our fortune/fate' (the provided senses list does not match this literary sense). بر نگیرد is composed of preposition بر + نگیرد (present/subjunctive negative of گرفتن) meaning 'does not seize/take hold of'. فروغ is the object 'radiance/glory'. Grammatically this is a protasis: 'If our fortune does not seize glory...'.
segments.s310-g16: همه چاره is the subject phrase 'all (the) remedy/means'. بادست is a fused form (likely ba-dast 'in hand / at hand') but the provided sense list does not capture this meaning; syntactically it qualifies the state of the remedies as uncertain/at hand. و links the two coordinated nouns; مردی دروغ is a noun + postmodifier: مردی here is 'manliness/valour' and دروغ 'false/illusory' qualifies it: 'bravery that is false'.
segments.s310-g233: به کردار is an adverbial phrase meaning “in the manner/way” (به + کردaر ‘conduct’). کرد is a past-tense verb (3rd sg.) ‘did/acted’. آنچه با ما بگفت is a relative clause ‘that which/he had told us’ (با ما = ‘to/with us’, بگفت = past ‘he said/told’), so the whole segment: ‘he acted in the manner of what he had told us.’
segments.s310-g234: که introduces reported content (‘that’). ما را places ‘us’ as the affected party (را = object/ topical marker). سپهر بلندست is a predication ‘the sky/heavens is lofty’ (بلندست = ‘is high/exalted’ fused form). جفت is a predicative noun ‘a pair/match’, so the clause means ‘that the high heavens are a match for us.’
segments.s310-g235: به کشتی = ‘by/with a ship’ (به marking means/instrument). به آب here marks direction/area ‘onto/over the water’. زره is the accompanying noun ‘armor’, and برگذشت is a past verb ‘passed over / crossed’. Together: ‘he crossed the water by ship, (wearing) armor.’
segments.s310-g236: همه رنج ما = ‘all our suffering’ (رنج as noun, ما marked as possessor). سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial phrase ‘completely/utterly’. باد here is the optative particle ‘may’ and گشت is the verb ‘become/turn’, so the sequence expresses a wish: ‘May all our troubles be wholly turned away / undone.’
segments.s310-g237: مرا (object ‘me’) with با نیا (literally ‘with/against the ancestor’ in older usage can mean ‘with anyone / the opponent’ — here it marks the party of address), جز = ‘except’, به خنجر = ‘by/with the dagger’ (instrumental), سخن = ‘speech/discourse’. Together: ‘As for me, there is no talk except by the dagger’ (i.e. only violence, no words).
segments.s310-g238: نباشد is a jussive/negative formula (‘let it not be’ / ‘there will not be’). نگردانم is 1st-person present/subjunctive with negative prefix (ن + گردانم) meaning ‘I will not turn/change/abandon’. این کین کهن = ‘this ancient hatred/vengeance’. Together the clause states emphatically: ‘I will not turn aside/abandon this old grudge.’
segments.s310-g239: به نیروی is a prepositional instrumental ‘by/with the power (of)’. یزدان is the divine name ‘God’, and پیروزگر is an epithet ‘triumphant/victorious’ modifying یزدان. The whole prepositional phrase gives the force/cause: ‘by the triumphant power of God.’
segments.s310-g240: ببندم is a 1st-person subjunctive/volitive of بستن ‘to bind’ (ب‑ prefix + stem + ‑م ’I’): ‘I will gird/bind’. به کین سیاوش is a prepositional phrase ‘with/for the vengeance of Siavash’ (کین = vengeance, سیاوش = proper name). کمر here means ‘waist/girdle’ and is used idiomatically ‘gird my loins’ = prepare for action. Together: ‘I will gird my loins with vengeance for Siavash.’
segments.s310-g217: بفرمود ('he commanded/said') is a past narrative/honorific verb; تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('that/so that'). مهتران هر کسی literally orders the noun (مهتران, 'chiefs/elders') with the distributive modifier هر کسی ('each person'), so the phrase means 'each of the chiefs'.
segments.s310-g218: تا…آرند is a subordinate clause headed by the verb آرند, a present/jussive/optative 3rd‑plural form here (commanded by تا = 'that they should bring'). به + آب + اندر expresses motion into the water ('into the water'), and کشتی بسی places the noun + quantifier after the verb phrase ('many/a sufficient number of ships').
segments.s310-g219: سوی is a preposition 'toward'; the adjective گنگ ('muffled/quiet/obscure') modifies دژ ('fortress'), forming the prepositional phrase 'toward the silent/muffled fortress'. بادبان is the direct object and برکشید is the past tense verb 'hoisted/raised', with the prefix بر‑ marking upward motion.
segments.s310-g220: به نیک و بدیها is a prepositional phrase 'to/into good and bad things' (i.e. 'in good and ill'); سر اندر کشید is an idiomatic verbal sequence where سر ('head' = oneself) + اندر ('into') + کشید (past 'drew/put') means 'gave/committed oneself' or 'submitted oneself'. The clause coordinates with the previous verb by describing the group's moral/fateful stance.
segments.s310-g221: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; آن جایگه شد = 'that place was made/established'. The two verbs بخفت and بخورد are past actions presented in sequence (ب‑ prefix is the older narrative marker here): 'he slept (بخفت) and ate (بخورد)', describing ordinary repose after the place was settled.
segments.s310-g222: برآسود is a perfect/stative verbal adjective 'was at rest / was relieved'. از introduces the source/cause ('from'), here linking روزگار نبرد ('the days/time of battle') as that from which he was relieved — 'freed from the time/period of war'.
segments.s310-g223: چنین گفت is the set phrase 'thus he said'. کایدر is an archaic/poetic locative/vocative particle meaning roughly 'here' or 'O you here'; بباشیم is the 1st‑person‑plural jussive/subjunctive 'let us be' (imperative-like), and شاد is the predicative adjective 'merry/happy' — together: 'Let us be merry here.'
segments.s310-g224: ز کار گذشته is a genitive phrase 'of/from past deeds/affairs' (ز = 'of/from', کار گذشته = 'past affairs'). نگیریم is the negative 1st‑person‑plural subjunctive/jussive 'let us not take/hold' (used idiomatically with یاد to mean 'let us not remember'), and یاد is the noun 'memory/recollection'.
segments.s310-g89: همه دشت مغز سر و خون گرفت is an NP + verb clause where همه دشت (“the whole plain”) is the theme and گرفت is an intransitive past used here to mean “was taken/was filled / became covered.” The list مغز سر و خون (brains, heads, and blood) is the material with which the plain is said to be filled; Persian allows such coordinated objects before the verb.
segments.s310-g90: This clause continues the scene with two coordinated nouns (دل سنگ رنگ — literally “heart stone-colour / heart like stone” — and طبر “axe”) sharing the same verb گرفت. دل سنگ‌رنگ is a poetic adjectival phrase (“heart hard/as-coloured as stone”); گرفت again functions intransitively here as “was stained / took on (blood).”
segments.s310-g91: سواران توران is the noun phrase “the horsemen of Turan.” که introduces a relative clause (who/that). روز درنگ places a time/condition—literally “during the day a delay” — i.e. “who had lingered/delayed by day.” درنگ is a noun meaning pause/delay used adverbially here.
segments.s310-g92: This gives what they possessed: داشتندی is an archaic 3rd-plural past form of داشتن “to have.” زبون here is adjectival (‘hapless/vanquished’) modifying شکار (prey), so the phrase says they “had as prey a hapless leopard” (شکار پلنگ). شکار is used as ‘prey/quarry.’
segments.s310-g93: ندیدند is the negative past 3rd-person plural of دیدن (“they did not see”). با + چرخ گردان is a prepositional phrase: چرخ here is the poetic word for the sky/heaven or the revolving wheel of fate, modified by گردان (“revolving”), and نبرد is the battle associated with that image—so literally “they did not see the battle with the revolving sky/heavens.”
segments.s310-g94: همی is a durative/adverbial particle (here ‘continually/kept’). خاک برداشت is a light verb construction where برداشت (from برداشتن) means “was/heaved up” or “rose” (the dust rose). از marks the source “from,” and دشت مرد means “the plain of men” (دشت = plain, مرد = men/persons).
segments.s310-g95: چو here is the conjunction “when.” کیخسرو is the subject. آن خاک و آن باد is a poetically repeated phrase (“that earth and that wind”); آن is the demonstrative ‘that’ repeated for emphasis, and دید is the past tense ‘saw/observed.’
segments.s310-g96: دل و بخت ایرانیان is the coordinated object ‘the hearts and the fortunes of the Iranians.’ شاد is an adjective ‘glad/happy’ that follows the noun phrase, and دید is used as a catenative past verb meaning “he saw (them) glad,” i.e. “he saw their hearts and fortunes rejoice.”
segments.s310-g209: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here the sense list given does not show the verbal sense); ملاح is the agent 'the helmsman' (subject) appearing after the verb in this V‑S‑O classical word order; کای شهریار is a vocative phrase ('O king') addressing the addressee. Together: 'To him said the helmsman, "O king..."'.
segments.s310-g210: بدین is a classical prepositional phrase 'به + این' meaning 'by/through this' (here split in the data); ژرف دریا is a noun phrase 'deep sea' with adjective before noun; نیابی is the negative present/subjunctive 2nd‑person form 'you will not find/you do not find' (ن + یابى), and گذار is the noun 'passage' that serves as the object of نیابی. Literally: 'By this deep sea you will not find a passage.'
segments.s310-g211: مرا is the dative/experiential 'for me' (here split in the token list); سالیان هست is an idiom meaning 'there are years' → 'it has been (years)'; هفتاد و هشت is the numeral 'seventy‑eight' that completes the temporal expression. Together: 'It has been seventy‑eight years for me.'
segments.s310-g212: ندیدم is the past negated verb 'I did not see'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that / when'; کشتی is the subject 'a ship'; بر او بر گذشت is a verbal phrase 'passed over it' where بر (here 'over, upon') + او ('it') and گذشت 'passed'. So: 'I have not seen that a ship passed over it.'
segments.s310-g213: بدو is 'to him' (prepositional pronoun); گفت is the past verb 'said' (the provided sense list does not include the verbal sense); پرمایه is an adjective compound (پر + مایه) meaning 'rich, full of resources' modifying the name; افراسیاب is the name 'Afrasiab'. The sequence presents an epithet + name as the speaker: 'to him said (the) rich‑of‑resource Afrasiab.'
segments.s310-g214: که introduces reported speech here. فرخ کسی is an exclamatory noun phrase 'fortunate/ blessed man' or 'how fortunate a man'; کو is an archaic/poetic relative/particle (here functioning like 'who'/'that'); بمیرد is the verb 'dies' (subjunctive/optative form 'who would die' or general present 'who dies'); در آب is the prepositional phrase 'in the water'. Together it yields an exclamation: 'Blessed is the man who dies in the water.'
segments.s310-g215: مرا 'me' (object) appears before the clause; چون here is causal 'since/because'; به شمشیر دشمن is an instrument/agent phrase 'by/with the enemy's sword' (به + instrument + agent); نکشت is the past negated verb 'did not kill'. The segment means 'since the enemy did not kill me with the sword.'
segments.s310-g216: چنانچون is a comparative 'just as/just so'; نکشتش is 'did not kill him' with object suffix -ش; نگیرد is a present/jussive 'will not seize/take' or potential negative ('does not / would not seize'); به مشت is the instrumental phrase 'by/with the fist'. So: 'Just as he did not kill him, he will not seize him with his fist.'
segments.s310-g57: This is a paratactic list of actions: برآمد is a past narrative verb 'appeared/sprang up', followed by a string of verbs joined by و (conjunction). گیر (seize), بر + بند (literally 'upon' + 'bind' — here a verbal action 'bind'), and کش ('pull/strike') form coordinated actions; the object is implied. The sequence of verbs linked by و is common in epic Persian to convey rapid successive deeds.
segments.s310-g58: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. با is the preposition 'with/by', linking the two means being negated (اسب تاب = 'horse' + تاب as endurance/movement; مرد هش = 'a man' + هش, an archaic/poetic qualifier). The phrase denies that the actions happened by either the horse's stamina or the man's prowess.
segments.s310-g59: از ایشان 'from them'; ز (poetic variant of از) + صد نامور 'of a hundred renowned' is a genitive-like specification; ده بماند is a numerical subject 'ten remained' where بماند (from ماندن) expresses that number being left/remaining. The structure uses two prepositions (از / ز) for emphasis in epic diction.
segments.s310-g60: کسی را که introduces a relative clause 'one whom/people whom ...' with را marking the (definite/emphatic) object. بد اختر 'an ill/unfavourable star' is the agent of misfortune, and بد براند (براند from راندن) means 'drives/ruins (badly)'. The relative clause modifies the preceding 'ten' — ten who were driven ill by an unlucky star.
segments.s310-g61: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'; آگاهی آمد is a light-noun + verb 'information/news came'. بر این رزمگاه (بر + این + رزمگاه) is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto this battlefield' positioned after the verb — common in Persian narrative word order.
segments.s310-g62: چنان خسته 'so/thus exhausted' is an adverbial + adjective; the following nominal group شاه توران سپاه reads as 'the army (سپاه) of the king of Turan' (شاهِ توران) with the head noun سپاه at the end. The Clause therefore states the extent of the army's exhaustion.
segments.s310-g63: Here که introduces a result/consequence clause ('so that/so as a result'); از خستگی 'from fatigue' gives the cause, جمله 'all' is the subject, and گریان شدند is a compound predicate: the participle گریان ('weeping') with شدند (become) to mean 'became weeping' or 'burst into tears'.
segments.s310-g64: ز (poetic fromِ) marks cause 'from'; درد دل شاه is a genitive phrase 'the pain of the king's heart' (دردِ دلِ شاه). بریان شدند uses the participle بریان ('roasted/consumed') with شدند to form a figurative predicate meaning 'were consumed/overcome (by grief)'.
segments.s310-g241: همه (all) + چین و ماچین here form a canned/poetic phrase meaning ‘everywhere / in every manner’; سپه is the direct object placed before the verb (classical Persian often allows object before verb), and گسترم is 1st person present/stative of گستریدن (‘I spread’ / ‘I will spread’). The subject 'I' is omitted and understood from the verb ending -م.
segments.s310-g242: به ... بر بگذرم is a directional phrase: به + noun marks the target/location ('into/onto the sea'), بر before the verb gives the sense 'over/onto', and بگذرم is 1st person present/subjunctive of گذراندن/گذر which here means 'I will pass (over)'. کیماک (clotted cream) is used metaphorically as a ‘sea’ (دریا) of richness.
segments.s310-g243: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; گردد is subjunctive/present of گشتن ('becomes/turns'); مرا is the object/dative 'for me' (here written as م + را in the word list — the particle را is the object marker attached to the pronoun). راست modifies 'ماچین و چین' meaning 'set right' or 'straight'; word order places the thing affected before the verb as is common in older Persian.
segments.s310-g244: بخواهیم is 1st person plural subjunctive/present used as a future/intent ('we will seek/ask for'); باژی is the object ('tribute' / 'booty' in context of the poem—note the provided dictionary senses list doesn't include 'tribute', so this is idiomatic/poetic use); ز مکران زمین is a prepositional phrase 'from the land of Makran' (ز = from, مکران = region, زمین = land).
segments.s310-g245: به + آب marks the medium/direction ('through/into the water'); زره ('armor') qualifies how the crossing will be done; بگذرانم is 1st person singular of گذراندن with causative sense 'I will cause/bring across'; سپاه is the affected object 'the army' and appears after the verb (object placement is flexible in poetic Persian).
segments.s310-g246: اگر introduces a conditional; چرخ گردان is a compound image for the revolving sky/fate (چرخ = heavens/fate, گردان = revolving); بود is the copula 'were/was' (here optative/conditional sense 'were'); نیک‌خواه is a predicative adjective/noun meaning 'well‑wishing' or 'benevolent', so the clause reads 'if the turning heavens were well‑disposed'.
segments.s310-g247: اگر starts a conditional; چند جایی is an adverbial phrase 'somewhere/a little bit somewhere' (چند = some/a little, جایی = place/anywhere); درنگ آیدم literally 'a delay comes to me' (درنگ = pause/delay, آیدم = 'comes to me' or 'I am met by'), so the whole expresses 'if I am delayed anywhere for a short while'.
segments.s310-g248: مگر is 'unless / except'; مرد خونی is 'a man covered in blood' or 'a bloody man' (خونی modifies مرد); به چنگ آیدم is an idiom: چنگ = grasp/hold (literally 'claw') and آیدم = 'comes to me', so 'falls into my grasp' or 'I seize/take him'. The clause thus restricts the delay: it would only happen if such a man is captured.
